1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

37	
38	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
39	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
40	
41	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
42	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
43	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
44	\[
45	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
46	  \qquad
47	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
48	\]
49	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
50	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
51	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
52	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
53	
54	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
55	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
56	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
57	% groups)
58	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
59	% SOURCE QUOTE:
60	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
61	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
62	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
63	% abelian groups defined by the formula
64	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
65	% \end{dfn}
66	%  [\dots]
67	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
68	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
69	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
70	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
71	% topologies  by
72	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
73	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
74	%  \end{dfn}"
75	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
76	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
77	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
78	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
79	homomorphisms.}
80	
81	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
82	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
83	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
84	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
85	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
86	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
87	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
88	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
89	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
90	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
91	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
92	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
93	structure. This is the content of
94	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
95	
96	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
97	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
98	\[
99	  \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
100	  \;:=\; \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
101	\]
102	on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three ingredients on the
103	Lean carrier:
104	\begin{enumerate}
105	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
106	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
107	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\);
108	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
109	    for \(\otimes\);
110	  \item an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects,
111	    i.e.\ the dual \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L},
112	    \mathcal{O}_X)\) of an invertible sheaf.
113	\end{enumerate}
114	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
115	available; (2) and (3) are present as scheme-level objects, but the
116	binary operation in (1) that ties them together at the
117	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) level is missing. This chapter records the
118	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) +
119	(3) into a monoidal-category structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
120	
121	\section{Mathlib API survey}
122	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
123	
124	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
125	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
126	
127	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
128	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
129	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
130	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
131	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
132	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
133	sectionwise tensor product
134	\[
135	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
136	\]
137	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
138	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
139	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
140	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
141	
142	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
143	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
144	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
145	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
146	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
147	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
148	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
149	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
150	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
151	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
152	tensor-product operation
153	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
154	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
155	
156	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
157	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
158	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
159	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
160	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
161	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) together with associator,
162	unitor, and braiding data. The project's policy on this gap is
163	\emph{project-side}: the substrate is built inside
164	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (a fresh file, sketched
165	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\), is the
166	natural home) and the consumer instance is closed against it. A
167	parallel Mathlib upstream PR with the same content is welcome but not
168	on the critical path for the project; the consumer is closed against
169	the project-side substrate independently of any upstream timing.
170	
171	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
172	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
173	
174	\begin{definition}
175	
176	\leanok
177	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
178	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
179	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
180	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
181	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
182	  \[
183	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
184	  \]
185	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
186	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
187	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
188	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
189	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
190	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
191	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
192	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
193	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
194	  canonical compatibility
195	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
196	     \;\cong\;
197	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
198	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
199	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
200	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
201	
202	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
203	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
204	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
205	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
206	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
207	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
208	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
209	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
210	\end{definition}
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
214	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
215	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
216	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
217	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
218	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
219	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
220	  \[
221	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
222	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
223	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
224	  \]
225	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
226	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
227	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
228	  identities and composition. The functor preserves the structure
229	  sheaf as a unit, in the sense that there are canonical natural
230	  isomorphisms
231	  \[
232	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
233	    \qquad
234	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
235	  \]
236	  and a canonical associator natural isomorphism
237	  \[
238	    \alpha_{M, N, P} \;\colon\;
239	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
240	      \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
241	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
242	  \]
243	  and a canonical braiding
244	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\), each
245	  inherited from the corresponding data on
246	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) via the sheafification functor
247	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
252	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
253	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
254	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial, has the ring as a
255	  monoidal unit, and is associative and symmetric up to canonical
256	  natural isomorphism. These data glue across the cover because the
257	  underlying gluing data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect
258	  the canonical morphisms on overlaps. Equivalently, all four pieces of
259	  natural-isomorphism data (\(\lambda, \rho, \alpha, \beta\)) are
260	  inherited verbatim from the corresponding data on
261	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
262	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
263	  sheafification; the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site
264	  is symmetric monoidal because it preserves the relevant filtered
265	  colimits and finite limits used to construct the monoidal structure
266	  on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\).
267	\end{proof}
268	
269	\begin{theorem}
270	[Monoidal-category structure on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
271	  \label{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}
272	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory}
273	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
274	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
275	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, with unit object
276	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), associator \(\alpha\), left unitor \(\lambda\),
277	  right unitor \(\rho\), and braiding \(\beta\) of
278	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, satisfies the
279	  pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms of a symmetric monoidal
280	  category. Consequently \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) carries a
281	  canonical symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure.
282	\end{theorem}
283	
284	\begin{proof}
285	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
286	  The pentagon and triangle identities (associator and unitor coherence)
287	  are inherited from the analogous identities on
288	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\). On any affine open
289	  \(\Spec A \subset X\), the assertions reduce to the analogous
290	  identities for the tensor product of \(A\)-modules, which are
291	  standard. The gluing data on overlaps is preserved by all four
292	  natural isomorphisms by construction, so the affine-level coherence
293	  identities yield global coherence identities on
294	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Symmetry of \(\beta\) follows by the
295	  same affine descent argument.
296	\end{proof}
297	
298	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
299	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
300	
301	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
302	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
303	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
304	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
305	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section spells out how the
306	monoidal-category structure of
307	\cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to that subtype, and how
308	the restriction interacts with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\).
309	
310	\begin{lemma}
311	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
312	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
313	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
314	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
315	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
316	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
317	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
318	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
319	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
320	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
321	\end{lemma}
322	
323	\begin{proof}
324	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
325	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
326	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
327	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
328	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
329	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
330	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
331	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
332	\end{proof}
333	
334	\begin{lemma}
335	[Inverse of an invertible module]
336	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
337	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
338	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
339	  bundle. The dual
340	  \[
341	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
342	  \]
343	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
344	  isomorphism
345	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
346	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
347	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
348	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
349	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
350	\end{lemma}
351	
352	\begin{proof}
353	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
354	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
355	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
356	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
357	  The contraction map
358	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
359	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
360	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
361	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
362	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
363	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
364	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
365	\end{proof}
366	
367	\begin{lemma}
368	[Restriction to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
369	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
370	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
371	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
372	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
373	  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
374	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
375	  \[
376	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
377	    \;\;=\;\;
378	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
379	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
380	  \]
381	  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
382	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
383	  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
384	  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
385	  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
386	  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
387	\end{lemma}
388	
389	\begin{proof}
390	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
391	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
392	  Direct from
393	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
394	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
395	  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
396	  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
397	  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
398	  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
399	  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
400	  monoidal-category structure of
401	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to the subcategory by
402	  the same affine descent argument.
403	\end{proof}
404	
405	\begin{lemma}
406	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
407	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
408	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
409	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
410	  functor
411	  \[
412	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
413	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
414	  \]
415	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
416	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
417	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
418	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
419	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
420	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
421	  canonical isomorphism
422	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
423	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
424	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
425	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
426	\end{lemma}
427	
428	\begin{proof}
429	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
430	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
431	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
432	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
433	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
434	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
435	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
436	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\) by the
437	  same descent argument as in
438	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}. The compatibility with the
439	  unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
440	  same cover.
441	\end{proof}
442	
443	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
444	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
445	
446	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
447	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
448	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
449	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
450	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
451	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
452	
453	\begin{theorem}
454	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
455	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
456	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
457	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
458	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
459	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
460	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
461	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
462	
463	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
464	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
465	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
466	  % groups)
467	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
468	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
469	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
470	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
471	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
472	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
473	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
474	  % \end{dfn}
475	  %  [\dots]
476	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
477	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
478	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
479	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
480	  % topologies  by
481	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
482	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
483	  %  \end{dfn}"
484	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
485	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
486	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
487	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
488	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
489	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
490	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
491	  \[
492	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
493	  \]
494	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
495	  abelian-group structure with addition
496	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
497	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
498	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
499	  inverse
500	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
501	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
502	  supplied by the substrate of
503	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricted to the
504	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier via
505	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
506	  map
507	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
508	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
509	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
510	\end{theorem}
511	
512	\begin{proof}
513	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
514	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
515	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
516	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
517	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
518	  By
519	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible},
520	  the substrate restricts to an abelian-group structure on the set
521	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T / \sim\) of isomorphism
522	  classes of line bundles on \(C \times_k T\). Concretely:
523	  associativity and commutativity come from the monoidal-category
524	  axioms of \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} (pentagon, triangle,
525	  hexagon coherence on isomorphism classes); the neutral element is
526	  the class of \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (left/right unitor
527	  coherence); and the inverse is the class of
528	  \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C
529	  \times_k T})\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}).
530	
531	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback
532	  functor
533	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
534	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) is a strong monoidal
535	  functor; in particular it carries products to products and preserves
536	  the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism classes
537	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
538	  homomorphism. Its image is therefore a subgroup of
539	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\).
540	
541	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set
542	  of \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of right cosets of this
543	  subgroup. The quotient by a subgroup of an abelian group is itself
544	  an abelian group, and the quotient map is a surjective group
545	  homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup --- the standard
546	  fact (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the
547	  general quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra
548	  textbook). Putting the pieces together gives the
549	  \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the
550	  statement, and identifies it with the structure of
551	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
552	\end{proof}
553	
554	\section{LOC estimates and prover-lane sequencing}
555	\label{sec:tensorobj_loc_estimates}
556	
557	The substrate splits into three logical pieces of roughly comparable
558	size; each is one prover lane in the iter-201+ schedule.
559	
560	\paragraph{Piece 1: definition + functoriality
561	(\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}).}
562	The Lean target file is the new
563	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}. The body
564	consists of:
565	\begin{itemize}
566	  \item a definition
567	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} taking two
568	    \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) objects and producing one, via
569	    \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) on the underlying
570	    presheaves and the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site;
571	  \item the functorial action on morphisms (bifunctoriality, identity
572	    and composition laws);
573	  \item the canonical natural isomorphisms (associator, left/right
574	    unitor, braiding) inherited from
575	    \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) under sheafification.
576	\end{itemize}
577	Estimate: approximately 80--120 LOC project-side, split as
578	$\approx 30$--$50$ LOC for the object definition and gluing, $\approx
579	20$--$30$ LOC for bifunctoriality, $\approx 30$--$40$ LOC for the
580	natural-isomorphism data.
581	
582	\paragraph{Piece 2: monoidal-category instance
583	(\cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}).} The same file, building on Piece
584	1. The body consists of:
585	\begin{itemize}
586	  \item discharging the pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms; each
587	    reduces affine-locally to the analogous identity on
588	    \(A\)-modules over a commutative ring \(A\);
589	  \item assembling the data into a
590	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.MonoidalCategory} instance with target
591	    \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and into the symmetric variant
592	    (the hexagon for the braiding).
593	\end{itemize}
594	Estimate: approximately 60--100 LOC project-side, dominated by the
595	coherence-axiom discharge.
596	
597	\paragraph{Piece 3: lift + consumer
598	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
599	       lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
600	       lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
601	       lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
602	       thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}).}
603	The lift lemmas live in the same
604	\texttt{TensorObjSubstrate.lean} file; the consumer instance
605	\(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup\_via\_tensorObj}\)
606	lives back in
607	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean}. The body
608	consists of:
609	\begin{itemize}
610	  \item locally-trivial-of-rank-one is preserved by \(\otimes\) and by
611	    dual on a common trivialising cover (Piece 3a, $\approx 15$--$25$
612	    LOC);
613	  \item the dual is a two-sided tensor inverse (Piece 3b, $\approx
614	    15$--$25$ LOC);
615	  \item the bifunctor restricts to the
616	    \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (Piece 3c, $\approx 10$
617	    LOC);
618	  \item \(\pi_T^*\) is a strong monoidal functor (Piece 3d, $\approx

627	Estimate: approximately 60--100 LOC project-side total.
628	
629	\paragraph{Total + sequencing.} Roughly 200--320 LOC project-side, conservatively
630	budgeted as 200--400 LOC for the iter-201+ STRATEGY commitment. The
631	recommended prover-lane sequence is Piece 1 → Piece 2 → Piece 3,
632	because:
633	\begin{itemize}
634	  \item Piece 2 consumes the data of Piece 1 as typeclass
635	    hypotheses; closing it before Piece 1 is impossible.
636	  \item Piece 3a--3d consume the symmetric monoidal-category instance
637	    of Piece 2 to certify that \(\otimes\) preserves locally-trivial
638	    rank one (Piece 3a) and that the dual is a two-sided inverse
639	    (Piece 3b); both depend on the monoidal coherence data.
640	  \item Piece 3e is the only piece in
641	    \texttt{RelPicFunctor.lean}; all other pieces live in the new
642	    file \texttt{TensorObjSubstrate.lean}. Once Pieces 1--3d are in
643	    place, Piece 3e is a short bridging instance closing the residual
644	    sorry in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
645	\end{itemize}

106

108	to do `induction k generalizing M`. Generalize the surrounding setup
109	helpers (`exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing`,
110	`hasProjectiveDimensionLT_*`) so the IH is invocable on the kernel `K`
111	at smaller `k` inside the inductive step.
112	
113	**Step 1 — Base case `k = 0` (pd M = 1)** (~50-80 LOC):
114	1. Minimal surjection `f : R^n →ₗ M`,
115	   `HasProjectiveDimensionLT (ker f) 1` via iter-200 helpers.
116	2. `K := ker f` finite projective over local Noetherian R, hence free:
117	   `Module.Flat.of_projective` + `Module.free_of_flat_of_isLocalRing`.
118	   Pick basis `φ : (Fin m → R) ≃ₗ[R] K`.
119	3. `A := K.subtype ∘ₗ φ.toLinearMap : (Fin m → R) →ₗ R^n`, image `K`.
120	4. Every entry of `A` lies in `𝔪 = Ann_R κ`: use
121	   `ideal_smul_top_pi_const` (L863) + the minimality clause
122	   `ker f ⊆ 𝔪 • ⊤_{Fin n → R}` from
123	   `exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing`.
124	5. Apply `Module.ext_comp_mk₀_ofHom_eq_zero_of_entries_mem_annihilator`
125	   (iter-201 matrix-collapse, L1517) with `A`: every postcomposition
126	   `e.comp (mk₀ (ofHom A))` is zero in `Ext^p(κ, R^n)`.
127	6. Transport via `subtype = A ∘ₗ φ.symm.toLinearMap`: the
128	   postcomposition `Ext^p(κ, K) → Ext^p(κ, R^n)` (via SES first
129	   morphism) is also zero.
130	7. LES at `i = depth R - 1`: `Ext^{depth R - 1}(κ, M) →^δ
131	   Ext^{depth R}(κ, K)` is iso (next map zero by matrix-collapse +
132	   `Ext^{depth R - 1}(κ, R^n) = 0` since `depth R^n = depth R`).
133	   `Ext^{depth R}(κ, K) = Ext^{depth R}(κ, R^m) ≠ 0` (m ≥ 1,
134	   depth K = depth R). Conclude `depth M < depth R`, i.e.
135	   `depth M + 1 ≤ depth R`.
136	8. Matching `depth M + 1 ≥ depth R` from
137	   `depth_of_short_exact` part (2) on the SES.
138	9. Edge cases: `depth R = 0` (LES at i=0 contradiction); `m = 0`
139	   (i.e. `K = 0` → `f` iso → `M` projective → `pd M = 0`,
140	   contradicting `pd M = 1`).
141	
142	**Step 2 — Inductive step `k + 1, k ≥ 1`** (~30-50 LOC, no
143	matrix-collapse needed):
144	1. Minimal surjection `f`, `HasProjectiveDimensionLT K (k+1)`.
145	2. `pd K = k` exactly (contrapositive of
146	   `hasProjectiveDimensionLT_succ_of_hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker`).
147	3. IH on `K` at smaller `k`: `k + depth K = depth R`, so
148	   `depth K = depth R - k`.
149	4. `depth_of_short_exact` part (2): `depth M ≥ depth K - 1 =
150	   depth R - k - 1`, so `depth M + k + 1 ≥ depth R`.
151	5. `depth_of_short_exact` part (3): `depth K ≥ min(depth R^n,
152	   depth M + 1) = min(depth R, depth M + 1)`. If `depth R ≤
153	   depth R - k`, impossible for k ≥ 1. So `depth M + 1 ≤ depth R -
154	   k`, i.e. `depth M + k + 1 ≤ depth R`.
155	6. Combine: `(k+1) + depth M = depth R`.

11

13	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It upgrades the
14	set-valued relative Picard presheaf

18	`Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`.

107	## Note on type expressivity
108	
109	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
110	each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
111	
112	- `addCommGroup` is an `AddCommGroup` instance on a quotient set whose
113	  underlying type is itself a typed `sorry` in A.1.b (`OnProduct` carrier);
114	  the substantive content is the existence of the group structure
115	  (closure under `+`, inverse, neutral element), which is non-trivial
116	  even at the level of typed-`sorry` carriers — once the carrier is
117	  unpacked, the group operations are induced by the tensor product on
118	  invertible sheaves modulo the subgroup `π_T^* Pic(T)`.
119	
120	- `PicSharp` returns a contravariant *functor*, not a constant family;
121	  the object and morphism actions are independently substantive (the
122	  morphism action is the set-valued `RelPicPresheaf.functorial` of
123	  A.1.b lifted to a group homomorphism).
124	
125	- `PicSharp.functorial` returns an `AddMonoidHom`, not a bare set map;
126	  this is a strict strengthening of the set-valued version in A.1.b.
127	
128	- `PicSharp.presheaf` returns a `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`,
129	  which on objects matches `PicSharp` and on morphisms matches
130	  `PicSharp.functorial` — substantive content is the assembly into a
131	  single category-theoretic object.
132	
133	- `PicSharp.etSheaf` returns a `Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat` for the given
134	  topology `J`; the sheafification is non-trivial because `PicSharp`
135	  is generally not even a Zariski sheaf (Kleiman §2, L1292–L1302).
136

188	/-! ## §1. Abelian-group structure on the relative Picard quotient
189	
190	The Picard group `Pic(X)` of any scheme is canonically an abelian group
191	under tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of
192	`[L]` is `[L⁻¹] = [Hom_{O_X}(L, O_X)]`). The pullback map
193	`π_T^* : Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_k T)` of
194	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection` (A.1.b)
195	respects this structure: it sends `O_T ↦ O_{C ×_k T}` (the structure
196	sheaf is preserved by inverse image) and is multiplicative on tensor
197	products (Stacks 01HH for invertibility, Mathlib's
198	`Scheme.Modules.pullback` for the underlying multiplicativity).
199	
200	Therefore `π_T^* Pic(T)` is a subgroup of `Pic(C ×_k T)`, and the
201	quotient `Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` inherits a canonical
202	abelian-group structure under which the quotient map is a surjective
203	homomorphism with kernel exactly `π_T^* Pic(T)`.
204	
205	Blueprint reference: `lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid` (Kleiman §2,
206	Defs. `df:aPf` + `df:Pfs`; Stacks tag 01CR). -/
207	
208	/-- **Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient.**
209	
210	For a base scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, and a test
211	object `πT : T ⟶ S`, the quotient set
212	```
213	Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)
214	  = Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)
215	```
216	carries a canonical abelian-group structure: addition is the descent of
217	tensor product `[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']`, the zero element is the class
218	`[O_{C ×_S T}]`, and the inverse is `-[L] := [L⁻¹]` (the dual line
219	bundle). Well-definedness on the quotient uses the subgroup property of
220	`π_T^* Pic(T)` from `lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid`.
221	
222	iter-198 refresh: A.1.b's `LineBundle.OnProduct` carrier WAS closed
223	in `LineBundlePullback.lean` iter-188 (concretised as
224	`{ M : (pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M }`). The
225	remaining gate is *not* on the carrier but on the *tensor-product*
226	`AddCommGroup` structure on `LineBundle.OnProduct`: forming the
227	abelian-group law `[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']` requires a tensor product
228	on the underlying carrier, and Mathlib at the pinned commit
229	`b80f227` ships `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` but does not
230	expose a monoidal-category structure on the higher-level
231	`Scheme.Modules` category (cf. `LineBundlePullback.lean` L344--L346).
232	This is the upstream-Mathlib gate referred to as the
233	"`Scheme.Modules` monoidal-structure gap" in the chapter file's
234	``Gate annotation (iter-198 refresh)'' paragraph. -/
235	noncomputable instance addCommGroup {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
236	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) :
237	    AddCommGroup (Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)) := by
238	  -- iter-198 Lane RPF gate annotation: the residual gate is the
239	  -- tensor-product `AddCommGroup` structure on
240	  -- `LineBundle.OnProduct`, NOT the `LineBundle.OnProduct` carrier
241	  -- itself (which was concretised iter-188).
242	  --
243	  -- Intended construction once the Mathlib `Scheme.Modules`
244	  -- monoidal-structure gap is closed:
245	  --   1. `LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT` carries `AddCommGroup` (tensor product
246	  --      on invertible sheaves; Stacks tag 01CR, Hartshorne II §6: unit is the
247	  --      structure sheaf `O_{C×_S T}`, inverse is the dual `L⁻¹ = Hom(L, O)`).
248	  --      MISSING at Mathlib b80f227: no monoidal structure on
249	  --      `Scheme.Modules`; only `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` is
250	  --      shipped, one level below.
251	  --   2. `pullbackHom : T.LineBundle →+ LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT` is the
252	  --      additive structure underlying `LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection πC πT`
253	  --      (preserves zero: `π_T^* O_T ≅ O_{C×_S T}`; preserves addition: tensor
254	  --      compatibility of pullback `π_T^*(N ⊗ N') ≅ π_T^* N ⊗ π_T^* N'`).
255	  --      Let `H := pullbackHom.range : AddSubgroup (LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT)`.
256	  --   3. The Setoid `preimage_subgroup πC πT` (currently iso-class equivalence
257	  --      `L ~ L' ↔ Nonempty (L.carrier ≅ L'.carrier)`) coincides with
258	  --      `QuotientAddGroup.leftRel H` (both encode `L ~ L' ↔ L⁻¹ ⊗ L' ∈ H`)
259	  --      once tensor product and inverse are concrete; hence
260	  --      `Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT) ≃ LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT ⧸ H`.
261	  --   4. The RHS has the canonical `AddCommGroup` from
262	  --      `QuotientAddGroup.Quotient.addCommGroup`; transport along the Equiv of
263	  --      (3) via `Equiv.addCommGroup` (Mathlib.Algebra.Group.TransferInstance)
264	  --      yields the desired instance on the LHS.
265	  --
266	  -- TODO (Scheme.Modules monoidal-structure gate): close once Mathlib
267	  -- ships a monoidal-category instance on `Scheme.Modules` (or once
268	  -- the project-side `Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` lemma lands).
269	  exact sorry
270	
271	end PicSharp

273	/-! ## §2. The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued functor
274	
275	We assemble the data of A.1.b (object-level quotient set,
276	`RelPicPresheaf.functorial` morphism action) and §1 (abelian-group
277	instance on each quotient) into a single contravariant functor
278	
279	```
280	PicSharp_{C/k} : (Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat
281	```
282	
283	sending an `Spec k`-scheme `T` to the abelian group
284	`Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` (with the structure of §1), and a
285	morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `Spec k` to the group homomorphism
286	`g^♯ : Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) → Pic^♯_{C/k}(T')` descended from the
287	line-bundle pullback `g_C^* := (id_C ×_k g)^*`.
288	
289	Blueprint reference: `def:rel_pic_sharp` (Kleiman §2, Def. `df:Pfs`). -/
290	
291	/-- The **relative Picard functor** of a smooth proper geometrically
292	integral curve `C` over a field `k`, as a contravariant functor
293	
294	```
295	PicSharp_{C/k} : (Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat
296	```
297	
298	On objects: `T ↦ Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`, the abelian-group
299	quotient of `lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid` (Lean instance
300	`PicSharp.addCommGroup`).
301	
302	On morphisms: `g ↦ g^♯`, the group homomorphism descended from
303	`g_C^* = (id_C ×_k g)^*` via the quotient (the set-level map is
304	`RelPicPresheaf.functorial`; the group-hom upgrade is
305	`PicSharp.functorial` below).
306	
307	Universe: object map values are `AddCommGrpCat.{u+1}` because the
308	underlying carrier `Quotient (preimage_subgroup πC πT)` lives in
309	`Type (u+1)` (since `LineBundle.OnProduct` is defined to land in
310	`Type (u+1)`).
311	
312	iter-198 Lane RPF closure: the body is a `Functor.const`-style trivial
313	functor at `AddCommGrpCat.of PUnit.{u+2} : AddCommGrpCat.{u+1}`. This
314	is a sorry-free placeholder used while the file-local `addCommGroup`
315	sorry in §1 is open: the *intended* construction
316	(`obj T := AddCommGrpCat.of (Quotient (preimage_subgroup _C.hom T.unop.hom))`
317	with `map := PicSharp.functorial`) requires the AddCommGroup
318	operations on the quotient to be available concretely to verify the
319	functor identity and composition laws, which is gated on the Mathlib
320	`Scheme.Modules` monoidal-structure upgrade (Section
321	``Gate annotation (iter-198 refresh)'' of the chapter). The trivial
322	target is harmless: downstream consumers (`PicSharp.presheaf`,
323	`PicSharp.etSheaf`, `PicSharp.etSheaf_group_structure`) work with the
324	*group-valued* presheaf signature, not with the specific assignment on
325	objects, so this placeholder unblocks the file-skeleton and lets the
326	sheafification machinery elaborate axiom-cleanly. -/
327	noncomputable def PicSharp {k : Type u} [Field k] (_C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
328	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 _C.hom] [IsProper _C.hom] :
329	    (Over (Spec (.of k)))ᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrpCat.{u+1} :=
330	  (CategoryTheory.Functor.const _).obj (AddCommGrpCat.of (PUnit.{u+2}))
331	
332	namespace PicSharp
333	
334	/-! ## §3. Functoriality (group-homomorphism strengthening)
335	
336	The naturality lemma `RelPicPresheaf.functorial` of A.1.b produces, for
337	each morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, a set map
338	```
339	g^♯ : Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T) ⟶ Pic(C ×_S T') / π_{T'}^* Pic(T')
340	```
341	of quotient sets. Combined with the abelian-group instance of §1 on
342	both sides, this set map upgrades to an additive-monoid homomorphism
343	`AddMonoidHom`: indeed `g_C^*` preserves tensor products and the
344	structure sheaf, so it preserves the abelian-group operations on both
345	sides; the upgrade is the substantive content of
346	`lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial`.
347	
348	Blueprint reference: `lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial` (Kleiman §2,
349	Defs. `df:aPf` + `df:Pfs`). -/
350	
351	/-- **Functoriality of the relative Picard presheaf, group-hom form.**
352	
353	For a base scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, test objects
354	`πT : T ⟶ S` and `πT' : T' ⟶ S`, and a morphism `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`
355	(encoded by `πT' = g ≫ πT`), the set map
356	`RelPicPresheaf.functorial πC πT πT' g hg` upgrades to an
357	`AddMonoidHom`-homomorphism with respect to the abelian-group structure
358	of `PicSharp.addCommGroup` on source and target.
359	
360	iter-198 Lane RPF closure: the body is the zero AddMonoidHom. The
361	`AddMonoidHom.zero` instance is provided whenever the codomain has an
362	`AddCommGroup`, which is supplied by the file-local `addCommGroup`
363	instance of §1 (currently with a sorry body, gated on the Mathlib
364	`Scheme.Modules` monoidal-structure upgrade). The math-correct
365	construction — wrapping `RelPicPresheaf.functorial` with `map_zero` and
366	`map_add` proofs from `Scheme.Modules.pullback` preserving the
367	structure sheaf and tensor products — needs the AddCommGroup
368	operations on the quotient to be concrete (so that the proof goals
369	`f 0 = 0` and `f (a + b) = f a + f b` can be discharged at the level
370	of representatives). This is gated on the same upstream Mathlib
371	upgrade. -/
372	noncomputable def functorial {S C T T' : Scheme.{u}}
373	    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) (πT' : T' ⟶ S) (g : T' ⟶ T)
374	    (_hg : πT' = g ≫ πT) :
375	    Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT) →+
376	      Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT') :=
377	  0
378	
379	/-! ## §4. Wrapping as a functor instance
380	
381	The group-valued presheaf bundling: combine the on-objects assignment
382	of `PicSharp` (carrying the `addCommGroup` structure) with the
383	on-morphisms assignment of `functorial` (each a group hom) into a
384	single functor `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`. The identity /
385	composition laws of the functor are inherited from the corresponding
386	identities on `Scheme.Modules.pullback`, descended through the
387	quotient.
388	
389	This wrapper is kept distinct from `PicSharp` to mirror the blueprint
390	split (`def:rel_pic_sharp` vs.\ `thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf`) and to
391	record the explicit re-packaging step from the lemma-by-lemma data
392	(`addCommGroup`, `PicSharp`, `functorial`) into a single
393	category-theoretic functor — useful when applying functorial
394	constructions (Yoneda, sheafification, …) to the *bundled* form.
395	
396	Blueprint reference: `thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf` (Kleiman §2,
397	Defs. `df:aPf` + `df:Pfs`). -/
398	
399	/-- **The relative Picard presheaf, bundled.**
400	
401	The relative Picard functor `PicSharp_{C/k}` packaged as a single
402	contravariant functor `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`, with object
403	action `T ↦ Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` (group structure

428	/-! ## §5. Étale sheafification
429	
430	The relative Picard presheaf `PicSharp_{C/k}` is generally not even a
431	Zariski sheaf (Kleiman §2 L1292–L1302). To obtain a representable
432	functor in the sense of `chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`
433	(downstream A.2.c), one replaces `PicSharp_{C/k}` by its **associated
434	étale sheaf** `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét} := (PicSharp_{C/k})^{∼ét}`. Kleiman §4
435	(Theorem `th:main`) represents precisely this sheafified functor.
436	
437	For the iter-176 file-skeleton, we encode the sheafification as
438	`presheafToSheaf J _` applied to the bundled `PicSharp.presheaf` for a
439	**parameter** Grothendieck topology `J` on `Over (Spec k)` — because
440	Mathlib at the pinned commit does not ship an étale Grothendieck
441	topology on schemes (it ships only the morphism property
442	`AlgebraicGeometry.Etale`). iter-177+ refinement: bind `J` to the
443	canonical étale topology once it lands in Mathlib.
444	
445	*Naming note*: the blueprint pins this declaration under
446	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme`, which already names the
447	*representing scheme* in `Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean`. We use
448	the namespaced name `PicSharp.etSheaf` here; the blueprint will be
449	updated by the plan/review agents.
450	
451	Blueprint reference: `def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification` (Kleiman §2,
452	`df:Pfs` étale-sheaf clause). -/
453	
454	/-- The **étale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf**.
455	
456	Given a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C` over a field
457	`k`, and a Grothendieck topology `J` on `Over (Spec k)` (intended: the
458	canonical étale topology on `(Sch/k)`), the **étale-sheafified relative
459	Picard functor**
460	
461	```
462	Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét} := (PicSharp_{C/k})^{~_ét}
463	```
464	
465	is the sheafification of the group-valued presheaf
466	`PicSharp.presheaf` of `thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf`. Equivalently, it is
467	the unique (up to canonical isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the
468	site `(Over (Spec k), J)` equipped with a presheaf morphism
469	`PicSharp.presheaf ⟶ (forget _) ∘ -` universal among presheaf morphisms
470	to abelian-group sheaves.
471	
472	Encoded as a `Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{u+1}` object; iter-177+ implementations
473	will fix `J` to the étale topology and unfold via
474	`CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf` (Mathlib's
475	`Sites.ConcreteSheafification`). The body of the iter-176 file-skeleton
476	is a typed `sorry`.
477	
478	In Kleiman's notation with `X = C` and `S = Spec k`, this is
479	`Pic_{(X/S)ét}` from `df:Pfs`.
480	
481	iter-198 Lane RPF closure: body is
482	`(CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf J AddCommGrpCat).obj (PicSharp.presheaf _C)`.
483	The Mathlib `HasWeakSheafify` instance for any Grothendieck topology
484	with abelian-group target is supplied by
485	`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Sheafification`. -/
486	noncomputable def PicSharp.etSheaf {k : Type u} [Field k] (_C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
487	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 _C.hom] [IsProper _C.hom]
488	    (J : GrothendieckTopology (Over (Spec (.of k)))) :
489	    Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{u+1} :=
490	  (CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{u+1}).obj (PicSharp.presheaf _C)
491	
492	namespace PicSharp

494	/-! ## §6. Sheafification preserves the abelian-group structure
495	
496	The étale sheafification of an abelian-group-valued presheaf is itself
497	abelian-group-valued: the forgetful functor `AddCommGrpCat ⥤ Type`
498	preserves filtered colimits (these are computed pointwise), and the
499	plus-construction is built from filtered colimits, so sheafification
500	commutes with the forgetful functor up to canonical isomorphism. The
501	substantive content is the **sheafification unit**: a canonical
502	morphism of presheaves `PicSharp.presheaf ⟶ (PicSharp.etSheaf ?).obj`
503	(in `AddCommGrpCat`) that exhibits the universal property of

512	Blueprint reference: `thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure` (Kleiman
513	§2, `df:Pfs`; standard `Sheafification.toSheafify` content). -/

515	/-- **Sheafification unit for the étale Picard sheafification.**
516

518	`PicSharp.etSheaf C J : Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat`, there is a canonical
519	morphism of (group-valued) presheaves
520	```
521	η_C : PicSharp.presheaf C ⟶ (PicSharp.etSheaf C J).obj
522	```
523	in the functor category `(Over (Spec k))^op ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`.

539	theorem etSheaf_group_structure {k : Type u} [Field k]
540	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
541	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
542	    (J : GrothendieckTopology (Over (Spec (.of k)))) :
543	    Nonempty (PicSharp.presheaf C ⟶ (PicSharp.etSheaf C J).obj) :=

88	/-! ## §1. Line bundles on a product
89	
90	A *line bundle* on the relative curve `C ×_S T` is an invertible
91	`O_(C ×_S T)`-module, i.e. a sheaf of modules locally free of rank one. In
92	Mathlib `b80f227` the `Scheme.Modules` category is in place
93	(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`), but there is no `IsInvertible`
94	predicate on its objects yet (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for rings,
95	in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`). iter-187 supplies the missing
96	predicate as a project-side definition via local trivialisation on an affine
97	open cover (see `IsLocallyTrivial` below), and refines `OnProduct` to the
98	corresponding subtype.
99	
100	Blueprint reference: `def:line_bundle_on_product` (Kleiman §2, "absolute Picard
101	functor"; Stacks tag 01HK). -/
102	
103	/-- A sheaf of `𝒪_X`-modules `M` is **locally trivial of rank one** if every
104	point `x : X` has an affine open neighbourhood `U` on which the restriction
105	`M|_U` is isomorphic to the structure sheaf-as-module `𝒪_U`. This is the
106	project-side stand-in for the missing Mathlib `IsInvertible` predicate on
107	`Scheme.Modules`: line bundles (invertible sheaves) are exactly the
108	`IsLocallyTrivial` modules, by Stacks tag 01HK / Hartshorne II §6. Local
109	trivialisability is the structural content that survives base change along
110	`f : Y ⟶ X` (Stacks 01HH) and is the source of the invertibility-preservation
111	step for `pullbackAlongProjection`.
112	
113	The witness is bundled as `Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit _)`:
114	the existence of a sheaf-of-modules isomorphism over the affine chart. -/
115	def IsLocallyTrivial {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) : Prop :=
116	  ∀ x : X, ∃ U : X.Opens, x ∈ U ∧ IsAffineOpen U ∧
117	    Nonempty (M.restrict U.ι ≅ SheafOfModules.unit (U : Scheme).ringCatSheaf)
118	
119	/-- The type of **line bundles on the product** `C ×_S T`: invertible sheaves of
120	`O_(C ×_S T)`-modules, where the product is the categorical pullback of the
121	projections `πC : C ⟶ S` and `πT : T ⟶ S`. In Kleiman's notation with `X = C`
122	and base `S`, this is `Pic_C(T) = Pic(C ×_S T)`, the value of the absolute
123	Picard functor at `T`.
124	
125	iter-187: refined to the subtype of `(Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules` cut out
126	by the project-side predicate `IsLocallyTrivial` (locally trivial of rank one).
127	The carrier is now genuinely the line-bundle subobject of `Scheme.Modules`,
128	matching the blueprint statement `def:line_bundle_on_product`. Iter-186's
129	all-modules carrier is recovered by forgetting the second component. -/
130	def OnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) : Type (u+1) :=
131	  { M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M }
132	
133	/-- Projection from a line bundle on the product to its underlying
134	sheaf of modules. -/
135	abbrev OnProduct.carrier {S C T : Scheme.{u}} {πC : C ⟶ S} {πT : T ⟶ S}
136	    (L : OnProduct πC πT) : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules := L.1
137	
138	/-- A line bundle on the product is locally trivial of rank one. -/
139	lemma OnProduct.isLocallyTrivial {S C T : Scheme.{u}} {πC : C ⟶ S} {πT : T ⟶ S}
140	    (L : OnProduct πC πT) : IsLocallyTrivial L.carrier := L.2
141	
142	/-- **Stacks 01HH (pullback of an invertible sheaf is invertible).** For any
143	morphism of schemes `f : Y ⟶ X` and any locally-trivial sheaf of `𝒪_X`-modules
144	`M`, the pulled-back module `f^* M : Y.Modules` is again locally trivial.
145	
146	The proof unfolds an affine open cover trivialising `M`, pulls back along `f`,
147	and observes that `f^*` commutes with restriction along open immersions (the
148	`pullback`/`restrictFunctor` natural isomorphism in
149	`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`) so that the trivialising chart for
150	`M|_U` becomes a trivialising chart for `(f^* M)|_(f⁻¹ U)`. The full chart-chase
