import Mathlib
import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.RelPicFunctor

/-!
# The `Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` substrate (A.1.c.SubT)

This file is the **A.1.c.SubT** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the
positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It records the dedicated
substrate on which the abelian-group instance of the relative Picard quotient
`Pic^♯_{C/k}(T) := Pic(C ×_k T) / π_T^* Pic(T)` rests (the residual `sorry`
of `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean`).

The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
`[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']` on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three
ingredients on the Lean carrier:

1. a binary tensor-product operation
   `⊗ : Scheme.Modules X × Scheme.Modules X → Scheme.Modules X`;
2. the structure sheaf `O_X` as a designated unit object for `⊗`;
3. an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects, i.e.
   the dual `L⁻¹ = Hom(L, O_X)` of an invertible sheaf.

At Mathlib's pinned commit (`b80f227`), only a presheaf-level version of (1)
is available (`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`); (2) and (3) are present
as scheme-level objects, but the binary operation in (1) that ties them
together at the `Scheme.Modules` level is missing, and there is no
`MonoidalCategory` instance on `Scheme.Modules X`. This file records the
project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) + (3)
into a monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X`.

## Status (iter-202 Lane TS — file-skeleton scaffold)

This file is the **iter-202 Lane TS** file-skeleton: each of the 4 pinned
declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex`) with a
`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-203+ work: the `tensorObj` definition lifts
`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through sheafification, and the consumer
`PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` then closes the residual `addCommGroup`
sorry of `RelPicFunctor.lean` L235.

The 4 blueprint-pinned declarations are:

1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj` (def) — the substrate binary
   operation `⊗ : Scheme.Modules X × Scheme.Modules X → Scheme.Modules X`,
   lifting `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` on underlying presheaves
   composed with sheafification on the small Zariski site.
   Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`.

2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality` (def) — the
   functorial action of `⊗` on morphisms: a pair `f : M ⟶ M'`, `g : N ⟶ N'`
   determines `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`.
   Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`.

3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory` (instance) — the
   monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X` with tensor `⊗`, unit
   `O_X`, associator, unitors, and braiding inherited from
   `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal` under sheafification.
   Per blueprint `thm:scheme_modules_monoidal`.

4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj` (def) — the
   `AddCommGroup` structure on the relative Picard quotient
   `Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)`, built via the
   `tensorObj` substrate. This is the iter-204+ closure target for the Lane RPF
   L235/L266-269 `addCommGroup` residual sorry.
   Per blueprint `thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj`.

Plus (PUSH-BEYOND) the supporting helper lemmas of the lift section
(`lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial`,
`lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible`, `lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct`,
`lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj`).

## References

Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex` (740 LOC,
4 pins). Source: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
Defs. `df:aPf` + `df:Pfs`; Stacks tags 01CR (Picard group), 03DM (relative
tensor product of `O_X`-modules). Mathlib module
`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules`
(`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj`).
-/

set_option autoImplicit false

universe u

open CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory

namespace AlgebraicGeometry

namespace Scheme

namespace Modules

/-! ## §1. The substrate tensor-product operation -/

/-- **The substrate operation `⊗` on `Scheme.Modules X`.**

For a scheme `X` and `M, N : X.Modules`, the tensor product
`M ⊗_X N : X.Modules` is the sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor
product `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` of the underlying presheaves of
`M` and `N` (affine-locally `(M ⊗_X N)(Spec A) = M(Spec A) ⊗_A N(Spec A)`).

Per blueprint `def:scheme_modules_tensorobj`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: the
body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body lifts
`PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` through the sheafification functor on
the small Zariski site of `X`. -/
noncomputable def tensorObj {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) : X.Modules :=
  sorry

/-- **Functoriality of `⊗_X`.**

A pair of morphisms `f : M ⟶ M'` and `g : N ⟶ N'` in `X.Modules` determines a
morphism `f ⊗ g : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N'`, compatible with identities
and composition; the assignment `(M, N) ↦ tensorObj M N` thereby extends to a
bifunctor `X.Modules × X.Modules ⥤ X.Modules` natural in both arguments.

Per blueprint `lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality`. iter-202 Lane TS
scaffold: the body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body inherits the
morphism action from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` under
sheafification. -/
noncomputable def tensorObj_functoriality {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
    (f : M ⟶ M') (g : N ⟶ N') : tensorObj M N ⟶ tensorObj M' N' :=
  sorry

/-! ## §2. The monoidal-category structure -/

/-- **Monoidal-category structure on `Scheme.Modules X`.**

The bifunctor `⊗_X` of `tensorObj_functoriality`, with unit object the
structure sheaf `O_X`, associator `α`, left/right unitors `λ`, `ρ`, and
braiding `β` inherited from `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal` under sheafification,
satisfies the pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms; hence `Scheme.Modules X`
carries a canonical (symmetric) monoidal-category structure.

Per blueprint `thm:scheme_modules_monoidal`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: the
body is a typed `sorry`; the iter-203+ body discharges the coherence axioms
affine-locally (each reduces to the analogous identity for the tensor product
of modules over a commutative ring) and assembles the
`CategoryTheory.MonoidalCategory` instance. -/
noncomputable instance monoidalCategory {X : Scheme.{u}} :
    MonoidalCategory (X.Modules) :=
  sorry

/-! ## §3. The lift through `LineBundle.OnProduct` (PUSH-BEYOND supporting lemmas)

The following helper lemmas record the lift of the substrate to the
locally-trivial subcategory used by the relative Picard consumer. They are not
`\lean{...}`-pinned in the blueprint (their statements are descriptive); the
typed signatures here scaffold the iter-203+ bodies. -/

/-- **Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial.**

If `M, N : X.Modules` are locally trivial of rank one (line bundles), so is
their tensor product `tensorObj M N`. Per blueprint
`lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: typed
`sorry`; the iter-203+ body trivialises both factors on a common affine cover
and uses that the tensor product of two free rank-one modules is free of rank
one. -/
lemma tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
    (hM : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial M) (hN : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial N) :
    LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial (tensorObj M N) :=
  sorry

/-- **Inverse of an invertible module.**

Every line bundle `L : X.Modules` has a two-sided tensor inverse: there is a
locally-trivial `Linv : X.Modules` (the dual `L⁻¹ = Hom(L, O_X)`) together with
a tensor isomorphism `L ⊗_X Linv ≅ 𝟙_ (X.Modules)`. Per blueprint
`lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: typed `sorry`;
the iter-203+ body builds the dual and the contraction isomorphism, which is an
isomorphism affine-locally on a trivialising cover. -/
lemma exists_tensorObj_inverse {X : Scheme.{u}} {L : X.Modules}
    (hL : LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial L) :
    ∃ Linv : X.Modules, LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial Linv ∧
      Nonempty (tensorObj L Linv ≅ 𝟙_ (X.Modules)) :=
  sorry

/-- **Restriction of `⊗` to the `LineBundle.OnProduct` carrier.**

For `S`-schemes `C, T`, the bifunctor `⊗_{C ×_S T}` restricts to the subtype
`LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT` of locally-trivial modules on `C ×_S T`, with unit
the structure sheaf and the dual as two-sided inverse. Per blueprint
`lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct`. iter-202 Lane TS scaffold: typed `sorry`. -/
noncomputable def tensorObjOnProduct {S C T : Scheme.{u}} (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S)
    (L L' : LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT) : LineBundle.OnProduct πC πT :=
  ⟨tensorObj L.carrier L'.carrier,
    tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial L.isLocallyTrivial L'.isLocallyTrivial⟩

end Modules

/-! ## §4. Consumer: the `AddCommGroup` instance on the relative Picard quotient -/

namespace PicSharp

/-- **Abelian-group structure on the relative Picard quotient via
`Scheme.Modules.tensorObj`.**

For a base scheme `S`, a curve-side morphism `πC : C ⟶ S`, and a test object
`πT : T ⟶ S`, the relative Picard quotient
`Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT) = Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`
carries a canonical `AddCommGroup` structure with addition the descent of the
tensor product `[L] + [L'] := [L ⊗ L']` of `Scheme.Modules.tensorObj`, neutral
element `[O_{C ×_S T}]`, and inverse `-[L] := [L⁻¹]`.

Per blueprint `thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj`. iter-202 Lane TS
scaffold: typed `sorry`. This is the iter-204+ closure target for the residual
`addCommGroup` sorry of `RelPicFunctor.lean` (L235); once this body lands, the
RPF instance closes against it. It is supplied as a `def` (rather than a global
`instance`) to avoid an instance diamond with the existing typed-`sorry`
`PicSharp.addCommGroup` instance in `RelPicFunctor.lean`. -/
noncomputable def addCommGroup_via_tensorObj {S C T : Scheme.{u}}
    (πC : C ⟶ S) (πT : T ⟶ S) :
    AddCommGroup (Quotient (RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup πC πT)) :=
  sorry

end PicSharp

end Scheme

end AlgebraicGeometry

Active prover mode: **mathlib-build**

## Your goal

Build project-local Mathlib infrastructure axiom-clean, one step at a time, going **as far as possible** in this iteration. Your output consists of definitions and lemmas that compile without `sorry` and whose `#print axioms` shows only `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`. You stop only when you hit a genuine mathematical blocker — not because one step is hard.

## Invariant

**No `sorry` in your output.** Every declaration you add must be fully proved. If you cannot close the current step: try alternatives first, then either prove a smaller sub-step or leave the declaration absent. Never add a declaration with `sorry`.

## Workflow

### 1. Orient

- Read `PROGRESS.md` for the objective: what infrastructure is needed, and why.
- Read the relevant blueprint chapter. It often contains the mathematical path to the ingredient you're building.
- Read `task_pending.md` for prior dead ends. Do not repeat them.

### 2. Scout the Mathlib API

Before writing any Lean, search for what already exists:

- `lean_local_search` for related names.
- `lean_leansearch` with a description of the mathematical content.
- `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns.
- Look for near-misses: a lemma that covers a sub-case, an instance you can compose, a `simp` set that contains what you need.

Even if you think you know the API — search anyway. Signatures change, instances disappear.

### 3. Build bottom-up, step by step

Start from the deepest missing piece identified in your scouting. For each step:

1. **Draft the type signature** and check it typechecks via `lean_diagnostic_messages` or `lean_run_code` before attempting the body.
2. **Write the proof.** Use Mathlib lemmas you verified exist. If a sub-step is missing, recurse: prove that sub-step first.
3. **Verify axiom-cleanliness** immediately after the step compiles: `#print axioms MyLemma`. Any `sorryAx` in the ancestry means the step is not axiom-clean — do not proceed until it is gone.
4. **Continue to the next step** in the chain.

Push as far as you can. Each axiom-clean step you add is permanent progress.

### 4. When stuck on a step — try before stopping

Before declaring a step impossible:

1. **Try a different proof route.** Reformulate the statement, weaken to a special case you can prove, use a detour through an equivalent form.
2. **Use the informal agent** if an API key is available (check `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` first): `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` "Prove [goal] using only current Mathlib." Formalize whatever it suggests. If no key is set, skip to step 3.
3. **Search more broadly.** The lemma you need might exist under a different name or in a namespace you haven't checked.
4. **Prove a strictly smaller sub-step** that is axiom-clean and genuinely useful — something that shrinks the remaining gap.

Only after exhausting these alternatives do you stop. **Comments describing an approach you haven't tried are not exhausted alternatives — attempt them first.**

### 5. Stopping

**Before stopping, run this self-review:**

1. Did I attempt every approach I wrote in a comment, TODO, or log entry? If not, go back and attempt them.
2. Are there adjacent declarations in the chain I could attempt with the infrastructure I just built?
3. Did I call the informal agent (or document that no key was available) before declaring the step impossible?
4. Is there any approach I thought of but skipped? Attempt it — a failed attempt with a named error is better than a comment.

Stop only when all four answers are "yes" or "not applicable." **Writing a comment about a possible approach counts as identifying it — which means you should attempt it.**

**You are fully authorized to make every mathematical and structural decision within your assigned file without waiting for the planner.** "I'll let the planner decide which route to take" is never a valid stop reason — pick the most promising route, attempt it, document the outcome. The planner corrects route choices next iter; an idle session corrects nothing.

Stop when you have tried alternatives and cannot make further axiom-clean progress. Before stopping:

- Commit all axiom-clean steps you completed.
- Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with a precise handoff (see Logging below).
- Ensure the file compiles with no `sorry`.

## File structure

Place new declarations under a clearly delimited section:

```lean
/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — <TopicName> -/
```

- Use `private` for helpers with no downstream use outside this file.
- Use `theorem` / `lemma` (non-private) for steps that other files will import.
- Add a one-line docstring to each non-private declaration explaining why it is project-local.

## Protected declarations

Read `archon-protected.yaml` before touching any existing declaration. You may add new declarations freely. You must not rename, re-type, or modify signatures of protected declarations.

## API alignment

Mathlib names and signatures are the most common failure source.

- Verify every name with `lean_local_search` or `lean_loogle` — do not rely on memory.
- Check that typeclass assumptions in your statement match what Mathlib requires.
- For `def`-backed types: Lean may not unfold them for typeclass synthesis; use explicit coercions or `change` / `show` in the proof body.

## Write permissions

Only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.

## Logging

Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror the `.lean` path):

```markdown
# Algebra/MathlibSupplement.lean

## Session summary
- Built axiom-clean: `Foo.bar`, `Foo.baz` (lines 12–40)
- Blocked on: `Foo.qux` — needs `Bar.aux` which requires [precise statement]

## Foo.bar (line 12)
- **Approach:** Direct from `Mathlib.Algebra.X` via `simp [Y]`
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean

## Foo.qux (not added)
- **Approach 1:** Tried [route] — FAILED because [reason]
- **Approach 2:** Tried informal agent — suggested [sketch], formalization blocked on [specific gap]
- **Next step:** Build `Bar.aux : [precise type statement]` first
- **Dead end:** Do not retry [route] — [why it fails]
```

One section per declaration attempted. For each: approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / NOT ADDED), dead-end warnings, next step.

## End-of-session handoff

**Write a `## Summary` section** before the verdicts: declarations added (count + names), declarations blocked (count + why), sorry count before → after across your file.

**Write a `## Why I stopped` section** — be brutally honest. Only axiom-clean Lean declarations count as progress. Comments, sketches, and log prose do NOT. If you wrote down approaches you didn't attempt, say so:

- `Real progress`: N axiom-clean declarations added — name each one with its line number.
- `Partial progress`: added some declarations, hit a specific blocker on the next — name the blocker precisely (not "it's complex", but the exact type or missing ingredient).
- `Approaches written but not attempted`: identified routes in comments or log but did not attempt them — name each. This is valid but weak; the planner will re-assign with a directive to attempt them.
- `Blocked — alternatives exhausted`: informal agent called (or key unavailable, documented), alternative routes tried, all failed — name what was tried, why each failed, and the exact type statement of the next needed ingredient.
- `Infrastructure already exists`: Mathlib has what was needed — cite the exact lemma name and namespace.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

11	/-!
12	# Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)
13	
14	This file is the **RR.1** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
15	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is
16	isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in `AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
17	
18	Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `WeilDivisor` on a scheme; adjacent pieces
19	(`MeromorphicOn.divisor`, `CommRing.Pic`, `Scheme.RationalMap`) cover different
20	ground. This file is therefore **project-bespoke**, scaffolding the formal-sum data
21	type `Div(X) = ⨁_{Y ⊂ X codim 1} ℤ` on a Noetherian integral scheme `X` satisfying
22	Hartshorne's condition `(*)`, the principal-divisor homomorphism
23	`div : K(X)^× → Div(X)` on a curve, the degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ` on a smooth
24	proper curve over an algebraically closed field, the degree-zero of principal divisors
25	on a complete nonsingular curve (Hartshorne Cor. II.6.10), and the linear-equivalence
26	relation `D ~ D'`.
27	
28	## Status (iter-172 file-skeleton)
29	
30	This file is the **iter-172 Lane C** file-skeleton: each declaration carries the
31	intended signature (matching the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin) with a `sorry` body.
32	The bodies are iter-173+ work after the sibling chapters `RR.2`
33	(`RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`), `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`), and `RR.4`
34	(`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`) land.
35	
36	The 9 pinned declarations are:
37	
38	1. `Scheme.WeilDivisor` — free abelian group on prime divisors (Definition).
39	2. `Scheme.RationalMap.order` — order of a rational function along a prime divisor.
40	3. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint` — Weil divisor associated to a closed point.
41	4. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree` — degree map over an algebraically closed base.
42	5. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom` — degree is a group homomorphism (Theorem).
43	6. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal` — principal divisor of a rational function.
44	7. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom` — `div` is a group homomorphism (Theorem).
45	8. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero` — `deg ∘ div = 0` on a complete curve.
46	9. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence` — linear equivalence of Weil divisors.
47	
48	## References
49	
50	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` (445 LOC, 9 pins).
51	Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, II §6 (pp. 130–137 + IV.1 pp. 294–296).
52	Stacks Project, tags 02RW (divisors), 02ME (order at a point), 0BE0 (degree),
53	0BE3 (principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
54	-/

60	open CategoryTheory Limits

62	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
63	
64	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
65	
66	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
67	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
68	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
69	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
70	`K(X)`.
71	
72	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
73	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
74	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
75	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
76	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
77	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
78	-/
79	
80	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
81	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
82	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
83	
84	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
85	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
86	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
87	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
88	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
89	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
90	integrality field is needed.
91	
92	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
93	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
94	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
95	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
96	  point : X
97	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
98	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
99	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
100	
101	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
102	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
103	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
104	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
105	
106	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
107	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
108	
109	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
110	
111	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
112	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
113	
114	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
115	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
116	
117	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
118	
119	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — PrimeDivisor open-immersion bridge
120	
121	Iter-200 Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 1 substrate (per `analogies/wd-stacks02iz.md`
122	and `task_results/mathlib-analogist-wd-stacks02iz.md`): on top of the existing
123	project-side `AlgebraicJacobian.Albanese.CoheightBridge`
124	(`Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding`, axiom-clean iter-183) we package the
125	PrimeDivisor-level open-immersion bijection used by future RR.1 / A.4.a
126	substrate work.
127	
128	The four declarations are:
129	- `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen` — given a prime divisor `Y` of `X` and
130	  `Y.point ∈ U`, the corresponding prime divisor of the open subscheme `U`.
131	- `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen` — push a prime divisor of an open subscheme `U`
132	  back to a prime divisor of the ambient scheme `X`.
133	- `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.equivOpen` — the bijection
134	  `{ Y : X.PrimeDivisor // Y.point ∈ U } ≃ U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor`.
135	- `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso` — the stalk identification along the
136	  open immersion `U.ι : U.toScheme ⟶ X`, a thin wrapper around Mathlib's
137	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso`.
138	
139	References: Stacks 02IZ (open-immersion stalks), Stacks 005X (coheight ↔ Krull
140	dim on Noetherian schemes); iter-183 substrate at
141	`AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`.
142	-/
143	
144	namespace Scheme.PrimeDivisor
145	
146	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
147	
148	/-- **Extensionality for `Scheme.PrimeDivisor`.** Two prime divisors with
149	the same underlying point are equal (the coheight witness is a `Prop`-valued
150	field, so it carries no data). Useful for the round-trip lemmas of the
151	`equivOpen` bijection below. -/
152	@[ext]
153	lemma ext {Y Y' : X.PrimeDivisor} (h : Y.point = Y'.point) : Y = Y' := by
154	  cases Y; cases Y'
155	  congr
156	
157	/-- **PrimeDivisor restriction along an open immersion.** Given a prime
158	divisor `Y` of `X` whose generic point lies in an open `U ⊆ X`, the
159	corresponding prime divisor of the open subscheme `U.toScheme`. The
160	codimension-one witness transports via `Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding`
161	(iter-183 project substrate, Stacks 02IZ topological side). -/
162	def restrictToOpen (U : X.Opens) (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
163	    (hYU : Y.point ∈ U) : U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor where
164	  point := ⟨Y.point, hYU⟩
165	  coheight := by
166	    rw [← Y.coheight]
167	    exact (Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding U.isOpen Y.point hYU).symm
168	
169	/-- **PrimeDivisor extension from an open subscheme.** A prime divisor of an
170	open subscheme `U.toScheme` lifts to a prime divisor of the ambient `X` along
171	the open immersion `U.ι`. Codimension-one is preserved via the same
172	`Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding` bridge, this time in the forward
173	direction. -/
174	def ofOpen (U : X.Opens) (YU : U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor) :
175	    X.PrimeDivisor where
176	  point := YU.point.1
177	  coheight := by
178	    rw [Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding U.isOpen YU.point.1 YU.point.2]
179	    exact YU.coheight
180	
181	@[simp]
182	lemma restrictToOpen_point (U : X.Opens) (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
183	    (hYU : Y.point ∈ U) :
184	    (restrictToOpen U Y hYU).point = ⟨Y.point, hYU⟩ := rfl
185	
186	@[simp]
187	lemma ofOpen_point (U : X.Opens) (YU : U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor) :
188	    (ofOpen U YU).point = YU.point.1 := rfl
189	
190	/-- **Bijection between prime divisors of `X` lying inside `U` and prime
191	divisors of `U.toScheme`.** The forward map is
192	`Y ↦ Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen U Y.val Y.property`; the inverse is
193	`Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen U`. Both maps are coheight-preserving by
194	`Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding`. -/
195	def equivOpen (U : X.Opens) :
196	    { Y : X.PrimeDivisor // Y.point ∈ U } ≃ U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor where
197	  toFun Y := restrictToOpen U Y.1 Y.2
198	  invFun YU := ⟨ofOpen U YU, YU.point.2⟩
199	  left_inv := by
200	    rintro ⟨Y, hY⟩
201	    rfl
202	  right_inv := by
203	    intro YU
204	    rfl
205	
206	/-- **Stalk identification along an open immersion at a prime divisor.** A
207	thin wrapper around Mathlib's `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso`
208	specialised to the underlying carrier of a prime divisor `Y` lifted from
209	the open subscheme. Stacks 02IZ stalk side. -/
210	noncomputable def stalkIso (U : X.Opens) (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
211	    (hYU : Y.point ∈ U) :
212	    U.toScheme.presheaf.stalk (restrictToOpen U Y hYU).point ≅
213	      X.presheaf.stalk Y.point :=
214	  AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso U ⟨Y.point, hYU⟩
215	
216	end Scheme.PrimeDivisor
217	
218	end AlgebraicGeometry
219	
220	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — `Ring.ordFrac` naturality
221	
222	Iter-201 Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 2 (HARD BAR + extras). Naturality of
223	`Ring.ord`, `Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom`, and `Ring.ordFrac` (Mathlib's
224	`K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` from `Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`) across a ring
225	isomorphism `e : R ≃+* S` between commutative rings, lifted to a compatible
226	fraction-field isomorphism `e_K : Frac R ≃+* Frac S`.
227	
228	This is the algebraic substrate consumed by iter-202+ Sub-build 3
229	(scheme-level `Scheme.RationalMap.order` naturality across the iter-200
230	`Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso` open-immersion bridge), which in turn closes
231	the non-zero branch of `rationalMap_order_finite_support` (the L535 sorry).
232	
233	References: Stacks 02RV (Hartshorne II.6.1), Stacks 02ME (DVR-of-stalk
234	characterisation at codim-1 points), Stacks 02IZ (stalk under open
235	immersion), Stacks 02MD (Mathlib's `Ring.ordFrac` / `Ring.ord` API).
236	-/
237	
238	namespace Ring
239	
240	/-- **`Ring.ord` naturality under a ring isomorphism.**
241	`ord R x = ord S (e x)`. The length of `R/(x)` as `R`-module equals the
242	length of `S/(e x)` as `S`-module: the `R`-linear equivalence
243	`R/(x) ≃ₗ[R] S/(e x)` (with the `S`-side equipped with `R`-module structure
244	via `e`) transports length across rings using `Module.length_eq_of_surjective`.
245	
246	Project-local because Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `ord` naturality lemma. -/
247	private lemma ord_ringEquiv {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [CommRing S]
248	    (e : R ≃+* S) (x : R) : Ring.ord R x = Ring.ord S (e x) := by
249	  have heq : Ideal.span ({e x} : Set S) = Ideal.map (e : R →+* S) (Ideal.span {x}) := by
250	    rw [Ideal.map_span, Set.image_singleton]; rfl
251	  letI alg : Algebra R S := e.toRingHom.toAlgebra
252	  haveI : IsScalarTower R S (S ⧸ Ideal.span ({e x} : Set S)) := by
253	    refine ⟨fun r s m => ?_⟩
254	    change (algebraMap R S r * s) • m = (algebraMap R S r) • (s • m)
255	    rw [mul_smul]
256	  let φ_ring : (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) ≃+* (S ⧸ Ideal.span ({e x} : Set S)) :=
257	    Ideal.quotientEquiv (Ideal.span {x}) (Ideal.span {e x}) e heq
258	  let φ : (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) ≃ₗ[R] (S ⧸ Ideal.span ({e x} : Set S)) := by
259	    refine { φ_ring.toAddEquiv with map_smul' := ?_ }
260	    intro r m
261	    induction m using Quotient.inductionOn with
262	    | h y =>
263	      change Ideal.Quotient.mk _ (e (r * y)) = e r • Ideal.Quotient.mk _ (e y)
264	      rw [map_mul]; rfl
265	  unfold Ring.ord
266	  rw [φ.length_eq]
267	  exact Module.length_eq_of_surjective (S := R) (R := S) (M := S ⧸ Ideal.span {e x})
268	    e.surjective
269	
270	/-- **`nonZeroDivisors` is preserved by a ring isomorphism.** Direct from
271	Mathlib's `MulEquivClass.map_nonZeroDivisors`. Project-local convenience
272	wrapper to phrase the result as an `Iff` instead of a `Submonoid.map` equality. -/
273	private lemma nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [CommRing S]
274	    (e : R ≃+* S) (r : R) :
275	    r ∈ nonZeroDivisors R ↔ e r ∈ nonZeroDivisors S := by
276	  rw [← MulEquivClass.map_nonZeroDivisors (e : R ≃* S)]
277	  refine ⟨fun h => ⟨r, h, rfl⟩, fun h => ?_⟩
278	  obtain ⟨r', hr', heq⟩ := h
279	  have : r = r' := e.injective heq.symm
280	  rw [this]; exact hr'
281	
282	/-- **`Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom` naturality under a ring isomorphism.**
283	`ordMonoidWithZeroHom S (e r) = ordMonoidWithZeroHom R r`. Reduces to
284	`ord_ringEquiv` after splitting on `r ∈ nonZeroDivisors R`. -/
285	private lemma ordMonoidWithZeroHom_ringEquiv {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [Nontrivial R]
286	    [CommRing S] [Nontrivial S] (e : R ≃+* S) (r : R) :
287	    Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom S (e r) = Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom R r := by
288	  unfold Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom
289	  simp only [MonoidWithZeroHom.coe_mk, ZeroHom.coe_mk]
290	  by_cases hr : r ∈ nonZeroDivisors R
291	  · have her : e r ∈ nonZeroDivisors S := (nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv e r).mp hr
292	    simp [hr, her, ord_ringEquiv e r]
293	  · have her : e r ∉ nonZeroDivisors S := fun h =>
294	      hr ((nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv e r).mpr h)
295	    simp [hr, her]
296	
297	/-- **`Ring.ordFrac` naturality** — iter-201 Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 2 HARD BAR
298	(per PROGRESS.md L108--L122 recipe step (1)).
299	
300	Given a ring iso `e : R ≃+* S` between Noetherian Krull-dim-`≤ 1` integral
301	domains and a compatible fraction-field iso `e_K : K_R ≃+* K_S` (compatibility
302	`e_K (algebraMap R K_R r) = algebraMap S K_S (e r)` for `r : R`), the
303	order-of-vanishing function on the fraction fields is invariant:
304	`ordFrac S (e_K x) = ordFrac R x` for any `x : K_R`.
305	
306	Proof: `x = 0` is the junk-zero branch. For `x ≠ 0`, write `x = mk' K_R a b`
307	with `a, b ∈ nonZeroDivisors R` via `IsLocalization.surj`. Then
308	`e_K x = mk' K_S (e a) (e b)` via the compatibility hypothesis, and both
309	sides reduce via Mathlib's `ordFrac_eq_div` to a quotient of
310	`ordMonoidWithZeroHom`-values, which agree by `ordMonoidWithZeroHom_ringEquiv`. -/
311	private lemma ordFrac_ringEquiv {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [IsDomain R]
312	    [IsNoetherianRing R] [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]
313	    [CommRing S] [IsDomain S] [IsNoetherianRing S] [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 S]
314	    {K_R K_S : Type*} [Field K_R] [Field K_S]
315	    [Algebra R K_R] [IsFractionRing R K_R]
316	    [Algebra S K_S] [IsFractionRing S K_S]
317	    (e : R ≃+* S) (e_K : K_R ≃+* K_S)
318	    (h_compat : ∀ r : R, e_K (algebraMap R K_R r) = algebraMap S K_S (e r))
319	    (x : K_R) :
320	    Ring.ordFrac S (e_K x) = Ring.ordFrac R x := by
321	  by_cases hx : x = 0
322	  · subst hx; rw [map_zero, map_zero, map_zero]
323	  obtain ⟨⟨a, b⟩, hab⟩ := IsLocalization.surj (nonZeroDivisors R) x
324	  have hb_nzd : (b : R) ∈ nonZeroDivisors R := b.2
325	  have hb_ne : (b : R) ≠ 0 := mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff_ne_zero.mp hb_nzd
326	  have h_inj_R : Function.Injective (algebraMap R K_R) := IsFractionRing.injective R K_R
327	  have hb_K_ne : algebraMap R K_R (b : R) ≠ 0 := by
328	    rw [Ne, ← map_zero (algebraMap R K_R), h_inj_R.eq_iff]
329	    exact hb_ne
330	  have ha_ne : a ≠ 0 := by
331	    intro ha
332	    subst ha
333	    rw [map_zero] at hab
334	    rcases mul_eq_zero.mp hab with hx' | hb_ne'
335	    · exact hx hx'
336	    · exact hb_K_ne hb_ne'
337	  have ha_nzd : a ∈ nonZeroDivisors R := mem_nonZeroDivisors_of_ne_zero ha_ne
338	  have he_b_nzd : e b ∈ nonZeroDivisors S := (nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv e b).mp hb_nzd
339	  have he_a_nzd : e a ∈ nonZeroDivisors S := (nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv e a).mp ha_nzd
340	  have hx_mk : x = IsLocalization.mk' K_R a ⟨b, hb_nzd⟩ := by
341	    rw [eq_comm, IsLocalization.mk'_eq_iff_eq_mul, hab]
342	  have he_K_x : e_K x = IsLocalization.mk' K_S (e a) ⟨e b, he_b_nzd⟩ := by
343	    rw [eq_comm]
344	    refine (IsLocalization.mk'_eq_iff_eq_mul).mpr ?_
345	    rw [← h_compat a, ← h_compat b, ← map_mul]
346	    exact congrArg e_K hab.symm
347	  rw [he_K_x, hx_mk]
348	  rw [Ring.ordFrac_eq_div S ⟨e a, he_a_nzd⟩ ⟨e b, he_b_nzd⟩]
349	  rw [Ring.ordFrac_eq_div R ⟨a, ha_nzd⟩ ⟨b, hb_nzd⟩]
350	  congr 1
351	  · exact ordMonoidWithZeroHom_ringEquiv e a
352	  · exact ordMonoidWithZeroHom_ringEquiv e b
353	
354	end Ring
355	
356	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
357	
358	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — Function field iso for open immersions
359	
360	Iter-201 Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 2 PUSH-BEYOND (step (2) per PROGRESS.md
361	L122--L138): canonical iso `U.toScheme.functionField ≅ X.functionField` for
362	integral `X` and nonempty open `U ⊆ X`, obtained by composing Mathlib's
363	`Scheme.Opens.stalkIso U (genericPoint U.toScheme)` with the transport across
364	the genericPoint equality `(U.ι (genericPoint U.toScheme)) = genericPoint X`
365	delivered by `genericPoint_eq_of_isOpenImmersion`.
366	
367	This iso is the bridge that lets `Ring.ordFrac_ringEquiv` apply at the
368	scheme level (with `e_K = Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso U` and `e =
369	Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso U Y hYU` for a prime divisor `Y` of `X` with
370	`Y.point ∈ U`). The full IsFractionRing-compatibility lemma chaining the
371	two isos is iter-202+ Sub-build 3 scope.
372	
373	Reference: Mathlib `AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField.genericPoint_eq_of_isOpenImmersion`
374	+ iter-200 `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso` wrapper. -/
375	
376	/-- **Function field isomorphism along an open immersion** (iter-201 Lane
377	WD-A4a Sub-build 2 PUSH-BEYOND). For an integral scheme `X` and a nonempty
378	open subscheme `U ⊆ X`, the function fields of `U.toScheme` and `X` are
379	canonically isomorphic. -/
380	noncomputable def Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
381	    (U : X.Opens) [Nonempty U] :
382	    (U.toScheme).functionField ≅ X.functionField :=
383	  (Scheme.Opens.stalkIso U (genericPoint U.toScheme)).trans
384	    (X.presheaf.stalkCongr (by
385	      have heq : ((genericPoint U.toScheme : U.toScheme.carrier).val : X.carrier) =
386	          genericPoint X :=
387	        genericPoint_eq_of_isOpenImmersion U.ι
388	      rw [heq]
389	      exact Inseparable.refl _))
390	
391	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
392	
393	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
394	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
395	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
396	
397	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
398	
399	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
400	
401	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
402	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
403	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
404	discrete valuation on `f`.
405	
406	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
407	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
408	
409	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
410	
411	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
412	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
413	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
414	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
415	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
416	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
417	
418	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
419	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
420	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
421	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
422	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
423	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
424	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
425	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
426	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
427	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
428	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
429	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
430	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
431	
432	end Scheme.RationalMap
433	
434	/-! ### Regular-in-codimension-one bridge class
435	
436	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` includes "regular in codimension one", i.e.\ every
437	prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk `O_{X,Y.point}`. Mathlib has no direct
438	typeclass for this; we package it as the project-bespoke class
439	`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne`, with an instance synthesising the per-`Y`

520	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
521	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]

523	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne U.toScheme]
524	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) (hYU : Y.point ∈ U)
525	    (e_K : U.toScheme.functionField ≃+* X.functionField)
526	    (h_compat : ∀ r : U.toScheme.presheaf.stalk
527	        (Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen U Y hYU).point,
528	      e_K (algebraMap _ U.toScheme.functionField r) =
529	        algebraMap _ X.functionField
530	          ((Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso U Y hYU).commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv r))

533	      Ring.ordFrac (U.toScheme.presheaf.stalk
534	        (Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen U Y hYU).point) f :=
535	  Ring.ordFrac_ringEquiv
536	    (Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso U Y hYU).commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv
537	    e_K h_compat f

539	/-! ### Order-on-curve algebraic identities
540	
541	iter-198 §2 substrate lemmas (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). These are
542	axiom-clean per-prime-divisor algebraic identities on
543	`Scheme.RationalMap.order` — direct consequences of `Ring.ordFrac` being
544	a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom composed with `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
545	(which is additive on nonzero arguments, junk-zero on `0`).
546	
547	The lemmas:
548	
549	- `Scheme.RationalMap.order_zero`: `ord_Y 0 = 0` (junk convention).
550	- `Scheme.RationalMap.order_mul_of_ne_zero`: `ord_Y (f·g) = ord_Y f +
551	  ord_Y g` when both `f, g ≠ 0`.
552	- `Scheme.RationalMap.order_units_inv`: `ord_Y u⁻¹ = -ord_Y u` for a
553	  unit `u : K(X)ˣ`.
554	
555	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` §2 (Hartshorne II §6 valuation
556	identities `v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f)+v_Y(g)`, `v_Y(f⁻¹) = -v_Y(f)`, `v_Y(1) = 0`). -/
557	
558	/-- `ord_Y 0 = 0` by the junk-on-zero convention.
559	
560	Direct from `Ring.ordFrac _` being a monoid-with-zero hom (`map_zero`)
561	and `WithZero.log_zero`. -/
562	@[simp]
563	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_zero
564	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
565	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
566	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)] :
567	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (0 : X.functionField) = 0 := by
568	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
569	  rw [map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
570	
571	/-- `ord_Y (f · g) = ord_Y f + ord_Y g` when `f, g ≠ 0`.
572	
573	Direct from `Ring.ordFrac _` being a monoid-with-zero hom (`map_mul`)
574	and `WithZero.log_mul` (which requires both factors nonzero). -/
575	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_mul_of_ne_zero
576	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
577	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
578	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
579	    {f g : X.functionField} (hf : f ≠ 0) (hg : g ≠ 0) :
580	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (f * g) =
581	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f + Scheme.RationalMap.order Y g := by
582	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
583	  rw [map_mul]
584	  exact WithZero.log_mul ((map_ne_zero _).mpr hf) ((map_ne_zero _).mpr hg)
585	
586	/-- `ord_Y f⁻¹ = -ord_Y f` for any `f : K(X)`.

588	The `f = 0` case is junk-vacuous (`f⁻¹ = 0` in a `GroupWithZero`, both sides

590	
591	Direct from `Ring.ordFrac _` being a monoid-with-zero hom (`map_inv₀`)
592	and `WithZero.log_inv` (which holds for all `WithZero (Multiplicative G)`
593	elements including zero, by the junk-on-zero convention). -/
594	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_inv
595	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
596	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)

598	    (f : X.functionField) :
599	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f⁻¹ = -Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
600	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
601	  rw [map_inv₀, WithZero.log_inv]
602	
603	/-- `ord_Y u⁻¹ = -ord_Y u` for a unit `u : K(X)ˣ`.
604

606	`((u⁻¹ : K(X)ˣ) : K(X)) = (u : K(X))⁻¹`, useful at call sites that thread

608	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_units_inv
609	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
610	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
611	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
612	    (u : (X.functionField)ˣ) :
613	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y ((u⁻¹ : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField) =
614	      -Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (u : X.functionField) := by
615	  rw [show ((u⁻¹ : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField) =
616	        (u : X.functionField)⁻¹ from by simp]
617	  exact Scheme.RationalMap.order_inv Y _

619	/-- **`ord_Y (-f) = ord_Y f`** for any rational function `f`.
620	
621	The argument: `(-f)^2 = f^2`, so taking `ordFrac` (a monoid-with-zero hom) and
622	then `WithZero.log_pow` gives `2 • ord_Y (-f) = 2 • ord_Y f` in `ℤ`. The free
623	action `(· • ·) : ℕ → ℤ → ℤ` cancels `2 ≠ 0`, giving the equality.
624	
625	iter-199 §2 substrate sharpening (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). Foundational
626	sign-flip identity for downstream consumers (Hartshorne II.6.10 non-constant
627	branch, ramification-inertia chase). -/

629	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_neg
630	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
631	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
632	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
633	    (f : X.functionField) :
634	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (-f) = Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
635	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
636	  have h : ((-f) ^ 2) = (f ^ 2) := by ring
637	  have h1 : (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) (-f)) ^ 2 =
638	      (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f) ^ 2 := by
639	    rw [← map_pow, ← map_pow, h]
640	  have h2 : ((Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) (-f)) ^ 2).log =
641	      ((Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f) ^ 2).log := by rw [h1]
642	  rw [WithZero.log_pow, WithZero.log_pow] at h2
643	  exact (smul_right_injective ℤ (by norm_num : (2 : ℕ) ≠ 0)) h2
644	
645	/-- **`ord_Y (f^n) = n · ord_Y f`** for a nonzero rational function `f` and
646	natural number exponent `n`.
647	
648	By induction on `n` from `order_mul_of_ne_zero` + `order_one`. The `f = 0`
649	hypothesis is necessary because the multiplicativity of `order` requires both
650	factors nonzero (otherwise `WithZero.log_mul` does not apply).
651	
652	iter-199 §2 substrate sharpening (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). Powers-of-`f`
653	identity used in the Hartshorne II.6.9 ramification-inertia chase
654	(`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` body) and in classical

656	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_pow_of_ne_zero
657	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
658	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
659	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
660	    {f : X.functionField} (hf : f ≠ 0) (n : ℕ) :
661	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (f ^ n) = n * Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
662	  induction n with
663	  | zero => simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
664	  | succ k ih =>
665	    rw [pow_succ, Scheme.RationalMap.order_mul_of_ne_zero Y (pow_ne_zero k hf) hf, ih]
666	    push_cast
667	    ring

750	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
751	
752	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
753	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
754	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
755	
756	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
757	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
758	
759	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
760	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
761	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
762	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
763	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
764	
765	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
766	
767	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
768	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
769	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
770	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
771	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
772	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
773	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
774	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
775	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
776	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
777	hypothesised regime. -/
778	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
779	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
780	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
781	
782	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
783	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
784	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
785	multiplicity one. -/
786	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
787	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
788	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
789	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
790	
791	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
792	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
793	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
794	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
795	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
796	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
797	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
798	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
799	
800	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
801	
802	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
803	
804	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
805	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
806	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
807	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
808	
809	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
810	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
811	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
812	
813	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
814	
815	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
816	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
817	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
818	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
819	
820	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
