1	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean
2	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
4	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
5	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
6	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
7	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
8	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
9	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
10	\section{Setup and motivation}
11	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
12	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
13	\[
14	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
15	  \,\colon\,
16	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
17	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
18	\]
19	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
20	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
21	Riemann--Roch dimension formula \(\ell(D) = \deg D + 1\) for \(\deg D \geq 0\) on
22	a genus-\(0\) curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
23	\(\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via \(1\) and a non-constant function
24	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)''
25	classification recovered as a degree-\(1\) morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) via
26	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
27	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
28	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
29	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
30	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
31	codimension-\(1\) cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
32	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
33	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
34	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
35	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
36	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
37	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
38	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
39	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
40	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
41	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
42	\(\mathrm{Div}(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \mathrm{codim} 1}} \mathbb Z\) on a
43	Noetherian integral scheme \(X\) satisfying Hartshorne's condition \((*)\), the
44	principal-divisor homomorphism \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on a
45	curve, the degree map \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) on a smooth proper
46	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
47	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
48	(\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
49	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
50	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on \(C\) defines a
51	finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
52	O_C(D)\( and the associated cohomology computation \)H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
53	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
54	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-\(0\) classification proper lives in
55	\texttt{RR.4}.
56	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
57	\((*)\):
58	% SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
59	% is regular in codimension one."
60	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional \((*)\).}
61	\[
62	  (\ast)\qquad
63	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
64	  codimension one.}
65	\]
66	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
67	additionally assume \(X\) is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
68	an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), where Hartshorne's condition \((*)\) is
69	automatic.
70	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
71	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
72	\((\ast)\) as a single typeclass. The chapter pins \((*)\) to a \emph{layered}
73	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
74	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
75	\begin{itemize}
76	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \ref{def:prime_divisor} and
77	    \ref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\), the degree
78	    \ref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
79	    \ref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
80	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
81	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
82	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of \(X\) already
83	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
84	    chain), so \(X\) has a unique generic point and the function field
85	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
86	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
87	    the bare formal-sum data type.
88	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\ref{def:order_at_point},
89	    \ref{def:principal_divisor}, \ref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
90	    signature additionally threads
91	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
92	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
93	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
94	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
95	    predicate ``for every \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) the
96	    stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as
97	    the conjunction
98	    $\forall x,\; \texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1 \Rightarrow
99	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173 introduced
100	    the \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} class
101	    (\ref{def:isRegularInCodimensionOne}) bundling this conjunction;
102	    the order/principal lemmas downstream take this class as a typeclass
103	    hypothesis when they require the DVR structure at codim-1 points.
104	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
105	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
106	    over \(\bar k\) with explicit integrality witness, namely
107	    \begin{quote}
108	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
109	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
110	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
111	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
112	    \end{quote}
113	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
114	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
115	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is
116	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
117	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
118	    properness; integrality threaded).
119	\end{itemize}
120	\noindent
121	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
122	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
123	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
124	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
125	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
126	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.
127	\section{Codim-\(1\) cycle group}
128	\begin{definition}
129	\leanok
130	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
131	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
133	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
134	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
135	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
136	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). A \emph{prime
137	  % divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral subscheme \(Y\) of codimension one."
138	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
139	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). A \emph{prime divisor} on \(X\) is a closed integral
140	  subscheme \(Y \subset X\) of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
141	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
142	  Y\( be its generic point\ldots''), \)Y$ is determined by its generic point
143	  \(\eta \in X\): closed integral subschemes of codimension one of \(X\) are in
144	  natural bijection with points \(x \in X\) such that the closure
145	  \(\overline{\{x\}} \subset X\) has codimension one. The closure
146	  \(\overline{\{x\}}\), equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
147	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
148	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
149	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
150	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
151	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
152	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
153	  the Mathlib predicate
154	  \[
155	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
156	  \]
157	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying \(X\).
158	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
159	  ``\(x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x\)'' (i.e.\ \(x \in \overline{\{y\}}\);
160	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
161	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
162	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
163	  preorder, so for the generic point \(\eta_Y\) of a prime divisor \(Y\) the
164	  only point strictly above \(\eta_Y\) is the generic point \(\eta_X\) of \(X\),
165	  and the chain \(\eta_Y < \eta_X\) has length one, giving
166	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta_Y\)\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
167	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
168	  integral'' is automatic from \(\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}\) being
169	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
170	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
171	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
172	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
173	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
174	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
175	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
176	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
177	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
178	  \begin{itemize}
179	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
180	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
181	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
182	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
183	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
184	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
185	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
186	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
187	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
188	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
189	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
190	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
191	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
192	      uses.
193	  \end{itemize}
194	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
195	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
196	  needs.
197	\end{definition}
198	\begin{definition}
199	\leanok
200	[Codim-\(1\) cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
201	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
202	  
203	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
204	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
205	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
206	  % condition \((*)\)) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
207	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
209	  % group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) generated by the prime divisors. We write a
210	  % divisor as \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\), where the \(Y_i\) are prime divisors, the
211	  % \(n_i\) are integers, and only finitely many \(n_i\) are different from
212	  % zero. If all the \(n_i \geq 0\), we say that \(D\) is \emph{effective}."
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
214	  Let \(X\) be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
215	  codimension one (i.e.\ \(X\) satisfies \((*)\)). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
216	  \[
217	    \mathrm{Div}(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
218	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
219	  \]
220	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of \(X\)
221	  (\ref{def:codim1_cycles}); an element \(D = \sum n_i Y_i\) with finitely
222	  many nonzero coefficients \(n_i \in \mathbb Z\) is called a \emph{Weil
223	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all \(n_i \geq 0\).
224	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
225	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
226	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
227	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor \(\to_0\) \(\mathbb Z\)}; integrality of \(X\) is
228	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (it becomes
229	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
230	  field appears). Hartshorne's \((*)\) is the standing convention in the
231	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
232	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis \((*)\) in the Lean encoding'' above
233	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
234	\end{definition}
235	\begin{definition}
236	\leanok
237	[Regular in codimension one]
238	  \label{def:isRegularInCodimensionOne}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne}
240	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II \S6, p.~130, the fourth clause of \((\ast)\)
241	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 147).
242	  % Stacks pointers: tag 0BX5 (regular schemes), tag 02ME
243	  % (DVR-of-stalk characterisation at codim-1 points).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\((\ast)\) \(X\) is a noetherian integral separated scheme
245	  % which is regular in codimension one."
246	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130 (the fourth clause of \((\ast)\)).}
247	  A scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in codimension one} when for every point
248	  \(x \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1\) (equivalently,
249	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\,x = 1\)) the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X,x}\) is
250	  a discrete valuation ring. The Lean encoding bundles this conjunction as
251	  a one-field \texttt{Prop} class so that downstream order / principal /
252	  degree lemmas may consume it as a typeclass hypothesis.
253	\end{definition}
254	\section{Open-immersion descent for prime divisors}
255	\label{sec:open_immersion_descent}
256	\textit{Source: Stacks Project tags 02IZ (stalk under open immersion) +
257	01J6 (open immersion preserves regularity); cross-referenced with the
258	project-local iter-183 substrate
259	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}.}
260	
261	The chapter's order / principal-divisor API is naturally stated on the
262	ambient scheme \(X\), but several downstream proofs (the
263	\(\texttt{rationalMap\_order\_finite\_support}\) non-zero branch in
264	particular) require localising to an affine open \(U \hookrightarrow X\),
265	identifying prime divisors of \(X\) lying in \(U\) with prime divisors of
266	\(U\), and transporting stalk-level data along the open-immersion stalk
267	isomorphism. This section records the substrate bijection
268	\(\{Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor} \mid Y.\texttt{point} \in U\}
269	 \;\simeq\; U.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\)
270	and the open-immersion descent of the \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\)
271	class.
272	
273	\begin{definition}
274	
275	\leanok
276	[Restriction of a prime divisor to an open subscheme]
277	  \label{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen}
278	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen}
279	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
280	  Given a scheme \(X\), an open subscheme \(U \hookrightarrow X\), a prime
281	  divisor \(Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\), and a witness \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U\),
282	  the restriction \(Y |_U : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) packages
283	  the point \(Y.\texttt{point}\) (viewed as a point of \(U\) via the open
284	  inclusion) with the coheight witness from
285	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight\_eq\_of\_isOpenEmbedding}\) (project-local
286	  substrate, iter-183 \texttt{CoheightBridge.lean} L175--L185).
287	\end{definition}
288	\begin{definition}
289	
290	\leanok
291	[Push of a prime divisor along an open immersion]
292	  \label{def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}
293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen}
294	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
295	  Given a scheme \(X\), an open subscheme \(U \hookrightarrow X\), and a
296	  prime divisor \(Y : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\), the push
297	  \(\texttt{ofOpen}\,U\,Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) packages the point
298	  \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U \hookrightarrow X\) (viewed as a point of \(X\))
299	  with the coheight witness from
300	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight\_eq\_of\_isOpenEmbedding}\) applied in the
301	  opposite direction.
302	\end{definition}
303	\begin{lemma}
304	
305	\leanok
306	[Bijection of prime divisors under open immersion]
307	  \label{lem:primeDivisor_equivOpen}
308	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.equivOpen}
309	  \uses{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen, def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}
310	  For an open subscheme \(U \hookrightarrow X\) of a scheme \(X\), there is a
311	  bijection
312	  \[
313	    \{Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor} \mid Y.\texttt{point} \in U\}
314	    \;\simeq\;
315	    U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}
316	  \]
317	  packaging the restriction (\ref{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen}) and the
318	  push (\ref{def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}) as mutual inverses. Both
319	  \texttt{left\_inv} and \texttt{right\_inv} hold definitionally
320	  (\texttt{rfl}) because both directions preserve the underlying point
321	  field via subtype eta + structure eta.
322	\end{lemma}
323	\begin{lemma}
324	
325	\leanok
326	[Stalk isomorphism across an open immersion]
327	  \label{lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso}
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso}
329	  \uses{def:primeDivisor_restrictToOpen}
330	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02IZ (stalk is invariant under restriction
331	  % to a non-empty open neighbourhood) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex).
332	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, tag 02IZ, plus Mathlib's
333	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}.}
334	  For \(Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) with \(Y.\texttt{point} \in U\), the
335	  stalk presheaf of \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\) at the restricted point coincides,
336	  up to canonical isomorphism, with the stalk presheaf of \(X\) at
337	  \(Y.\texttt{point}\). The Lean wrapper packages Mathlib's
338	  \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}\) for the project's
339	  \texttt{PrimeDivisor} bundle.
340	\end{lemma}
341	\begin{theorem}
342	
343	\leanok
344	[\(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\) descends along open immersion]
345	  \label{thm:isRegularInCodimensionOne_open}
346	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instOpen}
347	  \uses{def:isRegularInCodimensionOne, lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso,
348	    def:primeDivisor_ofOpen}
349	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\)
350	  and \(U \hookrightarrow X\) an open subscheme with
351	  \(\texttt{IsIntegral}\,U.\texttt{toScheme}\) (the latter is \emph{not}
352	  automatic from integrality of \(X\); it is threaded explicitly).
353	  Then \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\) also satisfies
354	  \(\texttt{IsRegularInCodimensionOne}\); the DVR-of-stalk property at a
355	  codim-1 point \(Y : U.\texttt{toScheme}.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) is
356	  transported from the corresponding point
357	  \(\texttt{ofOpen}\,U\,Y : X.\texttt{PrimeDivisor}\) on the ambient
358	  scheme via the stalk isomorphism (\ref{lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso})
359	  together with
360	  \(\texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing.RingEquivClass.isDiscreteValuationRing}\)
361	  on the ring-equiv image.
362	\end{theorem}

376	\begin{definition}[Function-field isomorphism along an open immersion]
377	  \label{def:functionFieldIso}
378	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso}
379	  \uses{lem:primeDivisor_stalkIso}
380	  \textit{Source: Archon-original packaging; built on Mathlib's
381	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso} +
382	  \texttt{genericPoint\_eq\_of\_isOpenImmersion}.}
383	  For an integral scheme \(X\) and a non-empty open subscheme
384	  \(U \hookrightarrow X\) with \(\texttt{IsIntegral}\,U.\texttt{toScheme}\),
385	  the function field of \(U.\texttt{toScheme}\) is canonically isomorphic
386	  to the function field of \(X\). The isomorphism is built by composing
387	  \(\texttt{Scheme.Opens.stalkIso}\,U\,(\texttt{genericPoint}\,U.\texttt{toScheme})\)
388	  with \(X.\texttt{presheaf.stalkCongr}\) along the equality
389	  \((\texttt{genericPoint}\,U.\texttt{toScheme}).\texttt{val}
390	   = \texttt{genericPoint}\,X\)
391	  coming from \(\texttt{genericPoint\_eq\_of\_isOpenImmersion}\,U.\iota\).
392	  This is the entry point Sub-build 3 (iter-202) uses to discharge

461	     = \texttt{order}\,(\texttt{restrictToOpen}\,U\,Y\,h)\,f'\)
462	    with \(f'\) the image of \(f\) under

464	    Direct consumer of Sub-build 2.

466	    Given a non-zero \(f \in X.\texttt{functionField}\):

484	specific project-local naturality / descent step; Sub-build 3 is a

495	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
496	\begin{definition}
497	\leanok
498	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
499	  \label{def:order_at_point}
500	  
501	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
502	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
503	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) of a prime
504	  % divisor; "zero/pole of \(f\) along \(Y\)") (read from
505	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
506	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
507	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its
508	  % generic point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at
509	  % \(\eta\) is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the
510	  % function field of \(X\). We call the corresponding discrete valuation
511	  % \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
512	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function
513	  % on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has
514	  % a \emph{zero} along \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\)
515	  % has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
516	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\)).}
517	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a prime divisor \(Y \subset X\) with generic
518	  point \(\eta \in Y\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
519	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
520	  function field \(K(X)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\)
521	  the \emph{order of \(f\) along \(Y\)} is the integer
522	  \[
523	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
524	  \]
525	  where \(v_Y\) is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
526	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\). If \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{zero of
527	  order \(\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}; if \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{pole
528	  of order \(-\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}.
529	  Specialisation to a curve. If \(C\) is a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)
530	  and \(P \in C\) is a closed point, then \(P\) is automatically a prime
531	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the \(1\)-dimensional
532	  scheme \(C\)); the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation
533	  ring by smoothness, and \(\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)\) is the standard DVR
534	  valuation of \(f\) at \(P\).
535	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
536	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
537	  \begin{quote}
538	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
539	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
540	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
541	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : \(\mathbb Z\)}
542	  \end{quote}
543	  with \emph{no} \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis (junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention,
544	  see below). The body is
545	  \[
546	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
547	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
548	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
549	      \bigr).
550	  \]
551	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
552	  \begin{itemize}
553	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K \(\to\!\!*_0\) \(\mathbb Z^{m_0}\)}
554	      (with \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)\)),
555	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
556	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
557	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
558	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-\(\leq 1\) ring to
559	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
560	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on \(r \in R\) is the
561	      length of \(R/(r)\) (the DVR valuation); on \(a/b \in K\), it is
562	      \(\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)\). The hypotheses
563	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
564	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
565	      synthesises from \([\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]\) (delivers
566	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
567	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
568	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
569	      X.functionField} via
570	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
571	      from \([\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]\) (delivers
572	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
573	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
574	      and from the explicitly threaded
575	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
576	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z\)}
577	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
578	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
579	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: \(\log 0 = 0\);
580	      \texttt{log\_mul}: \(\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y\) for
581	      \(x, y \neq 0\). This makes the downstream additive identities
582	      \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\), \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\),
583	      \(v_Y(1) = 0\) available as named lemmas for
584	      \ref{def:order_at_point}.
585	  \end{itemize}
586	  \emph{Junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention.} Setting \(f = 0\) in
587	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds \(0\) into
588	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
589	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps \(0\) to
590	  \(0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}\)); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
591	  \(\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0\). This is the canonical Mathlib junk
592	  choice; no separate \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis is threaded on the
593	  signature.
594	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
595	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
596	  topological condition \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1\)
597	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
598	  \(\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}\) on the
599	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
600	  the topological identity ``coheight of \(x\) in the scheme's
601	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
602	  corresponding to \(x\) in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
603	  ``ideal height \(=\) stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
604	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
605	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
606	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
607	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
608	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
609	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
610	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
611	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
612	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
613	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
614	\end{definition}
615	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
616	\begin{definition}
617	\leanok
618	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
619	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
620	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
621	  
622	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
623	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
624	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
625	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
626	  % be written \(D = \sum n_i P_i\), where the \(P_i\) are closed points, and
627	  % \(n_i \in \mathbf Z\)."
628	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If \(X\)
629	  % is a nonsingular curve, then \(X\) satisfies the condition \((\ast)\) used
630	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on \(X\). A prime divisor is just
631	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
632	  % P_i\(, where the \)P_i\( are closed points, and \)n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
633	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
634	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
635	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to \(P\)} is the element
636	  \[
637	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C)
638	  \]
639	  i.e.\ the prime divisor \(P\) with multiplicity one. The assignment is
640	  well-defined because the closed point \(P\) is automatically a codimension-\(1\)
641	  integral closed subscheme of the \(1\)-dimensional integral scheme \(C\).
642	  Conversely, every prime divisor on \(C\) is of this form, so an arbitrary
643	  divisor on \(C\) is a finite formal \(\mathbb Z\)-linear combination
644	  \(\sum n_i [P_i]\) of closed points.
645	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
646	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
647	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
648	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
649	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' at the
650	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
651	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
652	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
653	  codim-\(1\) regime:
654	  \[
655	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
656	      \begin{cases}
657	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
658	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
659	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
660	      \end{cases}
661	  \]
662	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
663	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
664	  of codimension \(\geq 2\) on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
665	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). This
666	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
667	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
668	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
669	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
670	  \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
671	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
672	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme \(C\), \(P\) a
673	  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
674	  \([P] := 1 \cdot P \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is recovered by combining
675	  \ref{def:divisor_closed_point} with the chain
676	  \(\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\)
677	  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
678	  one-dimensional-integrality content).
679	\end{definition}
680	\begin{lemma}
681	\leanok
682	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} in the codim-\(1\) regime]
683	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
684	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
685	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
686	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
687	  \(h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\) (the codimension-one witness
688	  promoting \(P\) to a \texttt{PrimeDivisor} via \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}),
689	  then
690	  \[
691	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
692	      \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1
693	      \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
694	  \]
695	\end{lemma}
696	\begin{proof}
697	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
698	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
699	  positive branch using the hypothesis \(h\).
700	\end{proof}
701	\begin{lemma}
702	\leanok
703	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} junk-branch]
704	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
705	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
706	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
707	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
708	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\) (the off-branch: \(P\) is not a
709	  codimension-one point of \(C\) --- e.g.\ \(P\) is the generic point of an
710	  integral scheme, or a higher-codim point of a higher-dimensional
711	  scheme), then
712	  \[
713	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
714	      0 \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
715	  \]
716	\end{lemma}
717	\begin{proof}
718	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
719	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
720	  negative branch using the hypothesis
721	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\).
722	\end{proof}
723	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
724	\begin{definition}
725	\leanok
726	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over \(\bar k\)]
727	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
728	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
729	  
730	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
731	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
732	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
733	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
734	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of \(D\) to be \(\sum n_i\)."
735	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
736	  nonsingular curve).}
737	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
738	  \(\bar k\). The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
739	  \mathrm{Div}(C)$ is the integer
740	  \[
741	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
742	  \]
743	  The definition uses that every closed point of \(C\) has residue field
744	  \(\bar k\) (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
745	  field \(k\) the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
746	  \(\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]\) recovers the
747	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
748	  \(\bar k\) specialisation.
749	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
750	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
751	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
752	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
753	  set ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
754	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
755	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on any scheme \(X\) the integer-valued sum of its
756	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) this coincides
757	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
758	  residue field \(\bar k\), so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
759	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
760	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
761	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
762	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
763	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
764	  (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
765	\end{definition}
766	\begin{theorem}
767	\leanok
768	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
769	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
770	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
771	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
772	  \(\bar k\). The degree map of \ref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
773	  homomorphism
774	  \[
775	    \deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
776	    \qquad
777	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
778	    \qquad
779	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
780	  \]

876	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime

879	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.

882	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.

1003	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`

1009	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
1010	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
1011	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
1012	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
1013	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
1014	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
1015	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
1016	iters of `RR.1`. -/

1018	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
1019	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
1020	    [IsIntegral C.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
1021	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
1022	    (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
1023	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 := by
1024	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10 (iter-178 partial). Case-split on whether every
1025	  -- prime-divisor order of `f` vanishes. On a complete nonsingular curve,
1026	  -- this case split exactly recovers Hartshorne's constant-vs-non-constant
1027	  -- split: `(∀ Y, ord_Y f = 0)` ⟺ `f ∈ k̄ \ {0}` (a nowhere-vanishing
1028	  -- rational function on a complete curve is constant; that direction is
1029	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10's "if `f ∈ k̄` then `(f) = 0`").
1030	  by_cases hconst : ∀ Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
1031	  · -- Constant branch: every order vanishes ⟹ `principal f hf = 0` ⟹
1032	    -- `degree (principal f hf) = degree 0 = 0`.
1033	    have hprincipal_zero : principal f hf = 0 := by
1034	      apply Finsupp.ext

1036	      change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0

1040	    exact Finsupp.sum_zero_index

1045	    -- morphism of curves multiplies degrees by `deg(φ)` (Hartshorne
1046	    -- II.6.9), so `deg(principal f hf) = deg(φ) · deg([0] - [∞]) =

1054	/-! ## §6. Positive part of a Weil divisor
1055	
1056	Iter-190 plan-phase Lane I Pin 2 corrective substrate. The carrier
1057	`X.WeilDivisor = X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ` is a finitely supported integer-valued
1058	function on prime divisors; the *positive part* `(D)_0` is obtained by
1059	replacing each coefficient `n_Y` with `max(n_Y, 0)`. Equivalently this is
1060	the lattice join `D ⊔ 0` in the pointwise lattice structure (the
1061	`semilatticeSup` instance on `Finsupp` is noncomputable). The complementary
1062	*negative part* `(D)_∞ := (-D)_0` then gives the canonical decomposition
1063	`D = (D)_0 - (D)_∞` into a difference of effective divisors.
1064	
1065	Blueprint reference: `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart` /
1066	`lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` of

1071	
1072	`positivePart D` is the divisor obtained from `D` by replacing each
1073	coefficient `n_Y` with `max(n_Y, 0)` (equivalently `D ⊔ 0` in the
1074	pointwise lattice structure on `Finsupp`). For `D = div(f)` on a smooth
1075	proper curve `C`, this recovers the divisor-of-zeros `(f)_0` — the
1076	formal sum of the zeros of `f` with their multiplicities.
1077	
1078	The complementary `negativePart D := positivePart (-D)` then gives
1079	`D = positivePart D - negativePart D` as a difference of effective
1080	divisors.

1082	Implementation: `Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp)`, the
1083	generic Mathlib packaging that maps a finsupp's coefficients through a
1084	zero-preserving function. (The `D ⊔ 0` lattice form is equivalent but

12	
13	We define the function field of an irreducible scheme as the stalk of the generic point.
14	This is a field when the scheme is integral.
15	
16	## Main definition
17	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.functionField`: The function field of an integral scheme.
18	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.germToFunctionField`: The canonical map from a component into the
19	  function field. This map is injective.

35	/-- The function field of an irreducible scheme is the local ring at its generic point.
36	Despite the name, this is a field only when the scheme is integral. -/
37	noncomputable abbrev Scheme.functionField [IrreducibleSpace X] : CommRingCat :=
38	  X.presheaf.stalk (genericPoint X)

42	    [h : Nonempty U] : Γ(X, U) ⟶ X.functionField :=
43	  X.presheaf.germ U
44	    (genericPoint X)
45	      (((genericPoint_spec X).mem_open_set_iff U.isOpen).mpr (by simpa using h))
46

57	  rw [injective_iff_map_eq_zero]

61	  cases Subsingleton.elim iU iV

/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — Sub-build 3 closure of `h_compat`

Iter-202 Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 3 (HARD BAR, both steps). We discharge the
`h_compat` hypothesis of `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ordFrac_stalkIso_naturality`
with `e_K := Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso U`, producing the user-facing
naturality `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.order_eq_order_restrict`:
`order Y (functionFieldIso U f) = order (restrictToOpen U Y hYU) f`.

Step 1 is the morphism-level commutativity in `CommRingCat`
(`Scheme.PrimeDivisor.functionFieldIso_compat`): the two ways of mapping the
stalk of `U.toScheme` at `Y` into `X.functionField` — first specialise to the
generic point of `U` then apply `functionFieldIso`, vs. first apply the
open-immersion `stalkIso` then specialise to the generic point of `X` —
agree. The proof is a germ-chase via `TopCat.Presheaf.stalk_hom_ext`,
`germ_stalkSpecializes`, and `Scheme.Opens.germ_stalkIso_hom`.

Reference: Stacks 02IZ (open-immersion stalks) + Mathlib's
`TopCat.Presheaf.germ_stalkSpecializes` + `Scheme.Opens.germ_stalkIso_hom`.
-/

/-- **Morphism-level compatibility for the function-field iso** (iter-202 Lane
WD-A4a Sub-build 3, step 1). For an integral scheme `X`, a nonempty integral
open `U`, and a prime divisor `Y` of `X` with `Y.point ∈ U`, the square
```
  stalk_U Y  --stalkSpec_U-->  functionField U
      |                              |
   stalkIso                     functionFieldIso
      v                              v
  stalk_X Y  --stalkSpec_X-->  functionField X
```
commutes in `CommRingCat`, where the horizontal maps are the canonical
`stalkSpecializes` maps to the respective generic points (i.e. the algebra
maps `O_{·,Y} → K(·)`). -/
theorem Scheme.PrimeDivisor.functionFieldIso_compat {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
    (U : X.Opens) [Nonempty U] [IsIntegral U.toScheme]
    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) (hYU : Y.point ∈ U) :
    U.toScheme.presheaf.stalkSpecializes
        ((genericPoint_spec U.toScheme).specializes (Set.mem_univ
          (Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen U Y hYU).point)) ≫
        (Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso U).hom =
      (Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso U Y hYU).hom ≫
        X.presheaf.stalkSpecializes
          ((genericPoint_spec X).specializes (Set.mem_univ Y.point)) := by
  apply TopCat.Presheaf.stalk_hom_ext
  intro V hxV

/-- **Naturality of `Scheme.RationalMap.order` across the function-field iso**
(iter-202 Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 3, step 2 — the HARD BAR endpoint). For an
integral locally-Noetherian scheme `X` regular in codimension one, a nonempty
integral open `U`, a prime divisor `Y` of `X` with `Y.point ∈ U`, and a
rational function `f` of `U.toScheme`, the order of the transported function
`functionFieldIso U f` along `Y` equals the order of `f` along the restricted
prime divisor `restrictToOpen U Y hYU`. This is the user-facing closure
consumer of Sub-build 2's `ordFrac_stalkIso_naturality`, with `h_compat`
discharged by the morphism-level `functionFieldIso_compat`. -/
theorem Scheme.PrimeDivisor.order_eq_order_restrict {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
    (U : X.Opens) [Nonempty U] [IsIntegral U.toScheme]
    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) (hYU : Y.point ∈ U)
    (f : U.toScheme.functionField) :
    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y
        ((Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso U).commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv f) =
      Scheme.RationalMap.order (Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen U Y hYU) f := by
  have hcompat : ∀ r : U.toScheme.presheaf.stalk
      (Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen U Y hYU).point,
      (Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso U).commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv
          (algebraMap _ U.toScheme.functionField r) =
        algebraMap _ X.functionField
          ((Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso U Y hYU).commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv r) := by
    intro r
    sorry
  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
  rw [Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ordFrac_stalkIso_naturality U Y hYU
      (Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso U).commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv hcompat f]

# AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean

## Session summary (iter-202, Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 3)
- Built axiom-clean: `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.functionFieldIso_compat` (step 1, morphism-level),
  `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.order_eq_order_restrict` (step 2, HARD BAR endpoint).
- **HARD BAR MET**: both step (1) AND step (2) closed axiom-clean (kernel triple only).
- Sorry count: **78 → 78 across project; file local sorries unchanged** (no new sorry added;
  the 3 pre-existing bodied sorries — `rationalMap_order_finite_support` non-zero branch,
  `principal_degree_zero` non-constant branch, and the Hartshorne II.6.9 `[∞]` typed pin —
  are all USER-blocked / off-limits and were not touched).
- Verdict: **Real progress** — 2 axiom-clean declarations added.

## `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.functionFieldIso_compat` (≈ lines 572–592)
- **Statement (morphism-level, in `CommRingCat`)**: for integral `X`, nonempty integral
  open `U`, prime divisor `Y` with `Y.point ∈ U`,
  `stalkSpec_U (gp_U ⤳ Y) ≫ functionFieldIso U .hom
     = (stalkIso U Y hYU).hom ≫ stalkSpec_X (gp_X ⤳ Y.point)`
  where the horizontal maps are the canonical `stalkSpecializes` maps to the respective
  generic points (= the algebra maps `O_{·,Y} → K(·)` of `stalkFunctionFieldAlgebra`).
- **Approach**: germ-chase via `TopCat.Presheaf.stalk_hom_ext`. After `intro V hxV`:
  - local `hcongr : (X.presheaf.stalkCongr e).hom = X.presheaf.stalkSpecializes e.ge := rfl`
    (no Mathlib `stalkCongr_hom` lemma exists; the `.hom` projection is `rfl`).
  - `simp only [Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso, Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso, Iso.trans_hom,
    restrictToOpen_point, hcongr]` to unfold defs + normalize the point to `⟨Y.point,hYU⟩`.
  - `simp only [germ_stalkSpecializes_assoc, Scheme.Opens.germ_stalkIso_hom_assoc]` reduces
    both sides to `germ (U.ι ''ᵁ V) · ≫ stalkSpecializes ·`.
  - close with `(germ_stalkSpecializes _ _ _).trans (germ_stalkSpecializes _ _ _).symm`
    (term mode — plain `rw [germ_stalkSpecializes]` would NOT match the final
    `germ ≫ stalkSpecializes`; term-mode unifies via the expected type).
- **Result**: RESOLVED — `#print axioms` = `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`.
- **Dead-end note**: `rw [TopCat.Presheaf.germ_stalkSpecializes]` fails ("pattern not found")
  on the terminal `germ ≫ stalkSpecializes` even though it is syntactically present; use the
  term-mode `germ_stalkSpecializes _ _ _` instead.

## `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.order_eq_order_restrict` (≈ lines 594–621)
- **Statement**: `order Y (functionFieldIso U .commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv f)
     = order (restrictToOpen U Y hYU) f` for `f : U.toScheme.functionField`, under
  `[IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X] [IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] [Nonempty U]
   [IsIntegral U.toScheme]`. All downstream `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` /
  `IsRegularInCodimensionOne U.toScheme` / `IsLocallyNoetherian U.toScheme` instances are
  synthesized automatically (via `instKrullDimLEStalk`, `instOpen`, and Mathlib's
  open-subscheme instances).
- **Approach**: prove the pointwise `h_compat` from `functionFieldIso_compat`:
  `congrArg (fun φ => (CommRingCat.Hom.hom φ) r) functionFieldIso_compat`, then
  `simp only [CommRingCat.hom_comp, RingHom.coe_comp, Function.comp_apply]`. The resulting
  `happ` matches the goal **by `rfl`/defeq**: `e.commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv x = e.hom.hom x`
  is `rfl`, and `algebraMap (stalk x) functionField = (stalkSpecializes …).hom` is defeq
  (the `stalkSpecializes` proof args differ only by proof-irrelevant `· ∈ univ` witnesses).
  So `exact happ` closes `h_compat`. The main goal then follows by
  `unfold order; rw [ordFrac_stalkIso_naturality U Y hYU _ hcompat f]`.
- **Result**: RESOLVED — `#print axioms` = `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`.

## PUSH-BEYOND (terminal closure of `rationalMap_order_finite_support` non-zero branch)
- NOT attempted in code: SCOPE FENCE + USER block on signature strengthening
  (`[IsLocallyNoetherian X]` → `[IsNoetherian X]`/`[CompactSpace X]`). The existing
  in-file comment block on that sorry already documents the full resolution skeleton
  (affine-chart reduction + Dedekind height-1 finiteness). `order_eq_order_restrict` is
  exactly the open-chart naturality the terminal closure (iter-203) will consume:
  it lets `support (Y ↦ order Y f)` be pulled back to each affine chart `U_i`.

## Handoff for plan/review agents
- **Blueprint**: `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §3-Sub-build-3 (lines ~441–464) describes
  this work in prose but has **no `\lean{...}`-pinned environment** for the two new
  declarations. Recommend the plan agent add `\begin{lemma}…\lean{...}` environments for
  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.functionFieldIso_compat` and
  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.order_eq_order_restrict` so `sync_leanok` can mark
  them `\leanok`. Both are ready for `\leanok` (axiom-clean, no sorry).
- **iter-203 cascade**: with `order_eq_order_restrict` in place, the terminal closure of
  `rationalMap_order_finite_support` becomes a finite-affine-cover bound — but remains gated
  on the USER-blocked `[IsNoetherian X]`/`[CompactSpace X]` signature strengthening.

## Why I stopped
Real progress: 2 axiom-clean declarations added (`functionFieldIso_compat` lines 572–592,
`order_eq_order_restrict` lines 594–621). The assigned Sub-build 3 chain (step 1 mechanism +
step 2 sorry-reducing-direction endpoint) is fully closed and axiom-clean — the HARD BAR.
No further axiom-clean progress is available in-file: the only remaining open targets are the
USER-blocked / fenced sorries (signature strengthening or Route-C-paused), which I must not
touch. No unattempted approaches remain in comments.
